parts.content: Determine the limiting reactant in this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the amounts of both reactants in moles. $n(\mathrm{Zn}) = \frac{3.00}{65.38} = 0.0459\ \mathrm{mol}$ **AND** $n(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}) = 1.00 \times \frac{25.0}{1000} = 0.0250\ \mathrm{mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Determine the limiting reactant. $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ is the limiting reactant because the reactants react in a $1:1$ ratio and $0.0250 < 0.0459$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the stoichiometry
steps.content:
To find the limiting reactant, we first look at the balanced chemical equation. From the equation, we can see that 1 mole of $\mathrm{Zn}$ reacts with 1 mole of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$:

$$\mathrm{Zn(s)} + \mathrm{H_2SO_4(aq)} \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO_4(aq)} + \mathrm{H_2(g)}$$

This means the stoichiometric ratio is $1:1$. This ratio will allow us to compare the actual moles of each reactant directly to see which one will run out first.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Zinc
steps.content:
We calculate the amount of Zinc in moles using the formula for mass and molar mass. The molar mass ($M$) of Zinc is $65.38\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$.

Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$n(\mathrm{Zn}) = \frac{3.00\ \mathrm{g}}{65.38\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}} = 0.0459\ \mathrm{mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of Zinc by dividing mass by molar mass.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Acid
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the amount of sulfuric acid in moles using its concentration and volume. Note that we must convert the volume from $\mathrm{cm^3}$ to $\mathrm{dm^3}$ by dividing by $1000$.

Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = CV$$

$$n(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}) = 1.00\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}} \times \frac{25.0}{1000}\ \mathrm{dm^3} = 0.0250\ \mathrm{mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of Sulfuric Acid by multiplying concentration by volume in dm³.

steps.title: Compare and identify
steps.content:
Since the reaction ratio between $\mathrm{Zn}$ and $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ is $1:1$, we need equal amounts of moles for both to react completely. 

Comparing our calculated values:
- Moles of $\mathrm{Zn} = 0.0459\ \mathrm{mol}$
- Moles of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4} = 0.0250\ \mathrm{mol}$

Because $0.0250 < 0.0459$, the $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ will be completely consumed first. Therefore, **sulfuric acid ($\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$)** is the limiting reactant.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Zinc available
steps.content:
First, we need to find out how many moles of zinc ($n_{\text{Zn}}$) are actually present. We use the mass of zinc and its molar mass from the periodic table (which is $65.38\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ according to the markscheme).

Using the formula from the data booklet:
$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\begin{aligned} n_{\text{Zn}} &= \frac{3.00\text{ g}}{65.38\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n_{\text{Zn}} &= 0.0459\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation is a fundamental part of **Topic S1.4: The mole**.

ti84.instructions: Enter the mass divided by the molar mass.

desmos.instructions: Divide 3 by 65.38 to get the moles.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of Zinc.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Acid available
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the moles of sulfuric acid ($n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}$) available in the solution. Remember to convert the volume from $\text{cm}^3$ to $\text{dm}^3$ by dividing by $1000$.

Using the formula from the data booklet:
$$n = CV$$

$$\begin{aligned} n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} &= 1.00\text{ mol dm}^{-3} \times \frac{25.0}{1000}\text{ dm}^3 \\ n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} &= 0.0250\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Calculate the product of concentration and volume (in dm³).

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of Sulfuric Acid.

steps.title: Determine the required acid amount
steps.content:
To find the limiting reactant using the **Required vs Available** approach, we look at the balanced equation to find the molar ratio:
$$\mathrm{Zn} : \mathrm{H_2SO_4} = 1 : 1$$

If we want to react all $0.0459\text{ mol}$ of Zinc, we calculate how much acid is *required* based on this $1:1$ ratio:
$$\text{Required } n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 0.0459\text{ mol}$$

This concept is covered in **Topic R2.1: The amount of chemical change**.

steps.title: Compare and identify limiting reactant
steps.content:
Now we compare the **available** amount of sulfuric acid to the **required** amount:

- **Available acid:** $0.0250\text{ mol}$
- **Required acid:** $0.0459\text{ mol}$

Since we have less acid available than what is required to react with all the zinc ($0.0250 < 0.0459$), the sulfuric acid will run out first.

Therefore, **$\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ is the limiting reactant**.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Zinc
steps.content:
First, we calculate the amount in moles of Zinc ($n_{\text{Zn}}$). Using the mass of Zinc provided ($3.00\ \text{g}$) and its molar mass from the periodic table ($M = 65.38\ \text{g/mol}$), we use the formula:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$n(\text{Zn}) = \frac{3.00\ \text{g}}{65.38\ \text{g/mol}} \approx 0.0459\ \text{mol}$$

This calculation is covered in **Topic S1.4: The mole**.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of Zinc.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Sulfuric Acid
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the amount in moles of Sulfuric Acid ($n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}$) using its concentration and volume. Remember to convert the volume from $\text{cm}^3$ to $\text{dm}^3$ by dividing by $1000$.

$$n = CV$$

$$n(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4) = 1.00\ \text{mol\ dm}^{-3} \times \frac{25.0}{1000}\ \text{dm}^3 = 0.0250\ \text{mol}$$

This formula is also found in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of sulfuric acid.

steps.title: Determine theoretical product yield
steps.content:
To find the limiting reactant using the **Theoretical Product Yield** approach, we calculate how much product (Hydrogen gas, $\text{H}_2$) each reactant could produce if it were completely consumed.

From the balanced equation: $\mathrm{Zn} + \mathrm{H_2SO_4} \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO_4} + \mathrm{H_2}$
- The mole ratio of $\text{Zn} : \text{H}_2$ is $1:1$, so $0.0459\ \text{mol}$ of Zn would produce **$0.0459\ \text{mol}$ of $\text{H}_2$**.
- The mole ratio of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 : \text{H}_2$ is $1:1$, so $0.0250\ \text{mol}$ of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ would produce **$0.0250\ \text{mol}$ of $\text{H}_2$**.

highlights.text: $$\mathrm{Zn(s)} + 	ext{H}_2	ext{SO}_4	ext{(aq)} \rightarrow 	ext{ZnSO}_4	ext{(aq)} + 	ext{H}_2	ext{(g)}$$

steps.title: Identify the limiting reactant
steps.content:
The limiting reactant is the one that produces the **smaller** theoretical yield of product.

Since $0.0250\ \text{mol} < 0.0459\ \text{mol}$, the $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ produces less Hydrogen gas than the Zinc could. 

Therefore, **$\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ is the limiting reactant**.

parts.content: Calculate the volume of hydrogen gas produced at STP.
parts.markscheme:
- State the amount of hydrogen gas produced based on the limiting reactant. $n(\mathrm{H_2}) = 0.0250\ \mathrm{mol}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the volume of hydrogen gas at STP. $V = 0.0250 \times 22.7 = 0.568\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate moles of Zinc
steps.content:
First, we find the amount in moles of Zinc ($n_{\text{Zn}}$) using the mass provided ($3.00\ \text{g}$) and its molar mass from the periodic table ($M \approx 65.38\ \text{g\ mol}^{-1}$).

Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$n(\text{Zn}) = \frac{3.00}{65.38} = 0.0459\ \text{mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate moles of Zinc

steps.title: Calculate moles of Sulfuric Acid
steps.content:
Next, we find the amount in moles of sulfuric acid ($n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}$) using its concentration ($1.00\ \text{mol\ dm}^{-3}$) and volume ($25.0\ \text{cm}^3$). Remember to convert the volume to $\text{dm}^3$ by dividing by $1000$.

Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = CV$$

$$n(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4) = 1.00 \times \frac{25.0}{1000} = 0.0250\ \text{mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate moles of Sulfuric Acid

steps.title: Identify the limiting reactant
steps.content:
From the balanced equation, $1$ mole of $\text{Zn}$ reacts with $1$ mole of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. 

Comparing our values:
- $n(\text{Zn}) = 0.0459\ \text{mol}$
- $n(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4) = 0.0250\ \text{mol}$

Since we have fewer moles of sulfuric acid, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is the **limiting reactant**. According to the stoichiometry ($1:1$ ratio), the amount of hydrogen gas produced will be equal to the amount of the limiting reactant:

$$n(\text{H}_2) = 0.0250\ \text{mol}$$

steps.title: Calculate Hydrogen gas volume
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the volume ($V$) of hydrogen gas produced at STP. The IB data booklet defines the molar volume of an ideal gas at STP ($V_m$) as $22.7\ \text{dm}^3\ \text{mol}^{-1}$.

$$\begin{aligned} V &= n \times V_m \\ V &= 0.0250 \times 22.7 \\ V &= 0.568\ \text{dm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the result from the markscheme.

calculator.description: Calculate the final volume of gas

steps.title: Calculate moles of reactants
steps.content:
First, we need to find the amount in moles for both reactants to determine which one is limiting. We use the formulas $n = \frac{m}{M}$ for the solid and $n = CV$ for the solution.

For Zinc ($Zn$):
$$n(Zn) = \frac{3.00\text{ g}}{65.38\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0459\text{ mol}$$

For Sulfuric Acid ($H_2SO_4$):
Converting volume to $\text{dm}^3$: $25.0\text{ cm}^3 = 0.0250\text{ dm}^3$
$$n(H_2SO_4) = 1.00\text{ mol dm}^{-3} \times 0.0250\text{ dm}^3 = 0.0250\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Identify limiting reactant
steps.content:
Looking at the balanced equation:
$$\mathrm{Zn(s)} + \mathrm{H_2SO_4(aq)} \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO_4(aq)} + \mathrm{H_2(g)}$$

The molar ratio is $1:1$. Since we have fewer moles of $H_2SO_4$ ($0.0250\text{ mol}$) than $Zn$ ($0.0459\text{ mol}$), **sulfuric acid is the limiting reactant**.

Because the ratio of $H_2SO_4$ to $H_2$ is also $1:1$, the amount of hydrogen gas produced is:
$$n(H_2) = 0.0250\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Apply Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
Now we use the ideal gas law, $PV = nRT$, to find the volume ($V$). At **STP**, the conditions are $T = 273\text{ K}$ and $P = 100\text{ kPa}$. Using $R = 8.31\text{ J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} V &= \frac{nRT}{P} \\ V &= \frac{0.0250\text{ mol} \times 8.31\text{ J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} \times 273\text{ K}}{100\text{ kPa}} \\ V &\approx 0.568\text{ dm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

*(Note: While the markscheme uses the molar volume constant $22.7$, the Ideal Gas Law approach yields the same result when using the precise STP temperature of $273.15\text{ K}$)*.

ti84.instructions: Enter (0.0250 * 8.31 * 273.15) / 100 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar to find the volume in cubic decimeters.

calculator.description: Calculate the volume of hydrogen gas using the Ideal Gas Law equation rearranged for V.

parts.content: If the experiment yields 85% of the expected hydrogen gas, calculate the actual volume collected.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the actual volume of gas collected. $0.568 \times 0.85 = 0.483\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the limiting reactant
steps.content:
First, we need to find out how many moles of $\mathrm{H_2}$ gas we expect to produce. This depends on the limiting reactant. 

We calculate the moles of sulfuric acid ($n = CV$) and zinc ($n = \frac{m}{M}$):
$$\begin{aligned} n(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}) &= 1.00\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}} \times 0.0250\ \mathrm{dm^3} = 0.0250\ \mathrm{mol} \\ n(\mathrm{Zn}) &= \frac{3.00\ \mathrm{g}}{65.38\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}} \approx 0.0459\ \mathrm{mol} \end{aligned}$$

Since the molar ratio in the equation is $1:1$, sulfuric acid is the **limiting reactant**. Therefore, the theoretical yield of $\mathrm{H_2}$ is $0.0250\ \mathrm{mol}$.

This uses concepts from **Topic R2.1: The amount of chemical change**.

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of sulfuric acid.

steps.title: Calculate the theoretical volume
steps.content:
Now, we convert the theoretical moles of $\mathrm{H_2}$ into volume at STP using the molar volume ($V_{\mathrm{m}}$) from the data booklet.

$$V_{\text{theoretical}} = n \times V_{\mathrm{m}}$$
$$V_{\text{theoretical}} = 0.0250\ \mathrm{mol} \times 22.7\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}} = 0.5675\ \mathrm{dm^3}$$

(Note: If you used slightly different rounding in earlier parts, you might have $0.568\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ as your expected volume).

calculator.description: Calculate the theoretical volume of gas.

steps.title: Scale for actual yield
steps.content:
Finally, we apply the yield factor. The question states that the experiment only produces 85% of the expected amount.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Actual Volume} &= V_{\text{theoretical}} \times \text{Percentage Yield} \\ &= 0.5675\ \mathrm{dm^3} \times 0.85 \\ &\approx 0.482\ \mathrm{dm^3} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get $0.482\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ (the markscheme accepts $0.483\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ if $0.568$ was used).

calculator.description: Calculate the final actual volume.

highlights.text: yields 85% of the expected hydrogen gas

steps.title: Identify theoretical moles
steps.content:
First, we identify the theoretical amount of hydrogen gas produced based on the limiting reactant. From the balanced equation:
$$\mathrm{Zn(s)} + \mathrm{H_2SO_4(aq)} \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO_4(aq)} + \mathrm{H_2(g)}$$

The molar ratio between $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ and $\mathrm{H_2}$ is $1:1$. Given that we have $0.0250\ \mathrm{mol}$ of the limiting reactant $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ (calculated using $n = CV$), the **theoretical yield** of hydrogen is:

$$\text{Theoretical } n(\mathrm{H_2}) = 0.0250\ \mathrm{mol}$$

This calculation uses concepts from **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change** regarding limiting reactants and stoichiometry.

steps.title: Calculate actual moles
steps.content:
The question states the experiment has an 85% yield. We can find the **actual moles** of hydrogen collected by multiplying the theoretical moles by the percentage yield (expressed as a decimal).

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Actual } n(\mathrm{H_2}) &= \text{Theoretical } n(\mathrm{H_2}) \times \text{Yield} \\ &= 0.0250\ \mathrm{mol} \times 0.85 \\ &= 0.02125\ \mathrm{mol} \end{aligned}$$

This step applies the percentage yield concept found in **Topic R2.1.4**.

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.0250 * 0.85 into the main calculation window.

ti84.instructions: Enter the product of 0.0250 and 0.85 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

steps.title: Convert to actual volume
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the actual moles of $\mathrm{H_2}$ into a volume. Since gases are measured at Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP) in these IB problems, we use the **Molar Volume ($V_m$)** from the data booklet.

$$V = n \times V_{\mathrm{m}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Actual Volume} &= 0.02125\ \mathrm{mol} \times 22.7\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}} \\ &= 0.482375\ \mathrm{dm^3} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get **$0.482\ \mathrm{dm^3}$**. Note: The markscheme result of $0.483\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ occurs if you round the theoretical volume ($0.568\ \mathrm{dm^3}$) before applying the 85% yield.

gdc.instructions: Multiply 0.02125 by 22.7 in the calculation window.

calculator.description: Calculate the final volume using the molar volume constant.

steps.title: Determine theoretical moles
steps.content:
From the initial reaction conditions, we first identify the theoretical yield of hydrogen gas. 

Comparing the moles of reactants:
- Moles of $\text{Zn} = \frac{3.00\text{ g}}{65.38\text{ g/mol}} \approx 0.0459\text{ mol}$
- Moles of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 = 1.00\text{ mol dm}^{-3} \times 0.0250\text{ dm}^3 = 0.0250\text{ mol}$

Since the reaction ratio is $1:1$, the sulfuric acid is the **limiting reactant**. Therefore, the maximum (theoretical) yield of $\text{H}_2$ is $0.0250\text{ mol}$.

highlights.text: 25.0 cm^3 of 1.00 mol dm^-3 sulfuric acid

steps.title: Calculate actual moles
steps.content:
The question states that the experiment yields 85% of the expected amount. We calculate the actual moles of hydrogen gas produced ($n_{\text{actual}}$) by applying this percentage to our theoretical yield:

$$\text{Actual moles} = \text{Theoretical moles} \times \text{Yield percentage}$$

$$n_{\text{actual}} = 0.0250\text{ mol} \times 0.85 = 0.02125\text{ mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate actual moles of hydrogen.

highlights.text: yields 85% of the expected hydrogen gas

steps.title: Define Ideal Gas variables
steps.content:
To find the volume at Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP), we use the Ideal Gas Law equation from the data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

According to IB standards for STP:
- Pressure ($P$) = $100\text{ kPa} = 10^5\text{ Pa}$
- Temperature ($T$) = $273\text{ K}$ (or $273.15\text{ K}$)
- Gas constant ($R$) = $8.31\text{ J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$

steps.title: Solve for actual volume
steps.content:
Rearrange the Ideal Gas Law to solve for volume ($V$). To get the result directly in $\text{dm}^3$, we can use $P = 100\text{ kPa}$ and $n = 0.02125\text{ mol}$:

$$V = \frac{nRT}{P}$$

$$\begin{aligned} V &= \frac{0.02125 \times 8.31 \times 273}{100} \\ V &\approx 0.482\text{ dm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

(Note: Using the rounded theoretical volume of $0.568\text{ dm}^3$ from the markscheme: $0.568 \times 0.85 = 0.483\text{ dm}^3$. Both are acceptable).

desmos.instructions: Enter (0.02125 * 8.31 * 273) / 100 to find the volume in dm³.

calculator.description: Calculate the volume using the Ideal Gas Law values.

parts.content: Write a balanced chemical equation for the reaction.
parts.markscheme: $\mathrm{NaOH}(aq) + \mathrm{HCl}(aq) \to \mathrm{NaCl}(aq) + \mathrm{H_2O}(l)$ <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content:
The question describes a reaction between an acid and a base. This is a **neutralization reaction**, which follows a specific general pattern covered in **Topic R3.1.7** of the IB syllabus.

The general word equation for this pattern is:
$$\text{Acid} + \text{Base} \rightarrow \text{Salt} + \text{Water}$$

highlights.text: Sodium hydroxide reacts with hydrochloric acid in a neutralization

steps.title: Predict the salt produced
steps.content:
To find the chemical formula of the salt, we combine the **cation** (positive ion) from the base with the **anion** (negative ion) from the acid:

1.  The base is sodium hydroxide ($\mathrm{NaOH}$), which provides the sodium ion: $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$
2.  The acid is hydrochloric acid ($\mathrm{HCl}$), which provides the chloride ion: $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$

When these combine to form a neutral salt, the charges cancel out to give:
$$\mathrm{NaCl}$$

steps.title: Construct the chemical equation
steps.content:
Now we place the formulas into the general neutralization pattern:

$$\mathrm{NaOH} + \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaCl} + \mathrm{H_2O}$$

steps.title: Balance and add states
steps.content:
Finally, we check if the equation is balanced. 

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{Reactants:} && \text{Products:} \\ &1 \times \mathrm{Na} && 1 \times \mathrm{Na} \\ &1 \times \mathrm{Cl} && 1 \times \mathrm{Cl} \\ &2 \times \mathrm{H} && 2 \times \mathrm{H} \text{ (in } \mathrm{H_2O}) \\ &1 \times \mathrm{O} && 1 \times \mathrm{O} \text{ (in } \mathrm{H_2O}) \end{aligned}$$

The equation is already balanced. We add state symbols to indicate the substances are in aqueous solution ($aq$) and water is a liquid ($l$).

$$\mathrm{NaOH}(aq) + \mathrm{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}(aq) + \mathrm{H_2O}(l)$$

This matches the markscheme requirement for 1 mark.

steps.title: Identify reactant ions
steps.content:
To write the equation using the **double displacement approach**, we first identify the constituent ions in each reactant:

*   **Sodium hydroxide ($\mathrm{NaOH}$)**: Composed of the sodium cation ($\mathrm{Na}^{+}$) and the hydroxide anion ($\mathrm{OH}^{-}$).
*   **Hydrochloric acid ($\mathrm{HCl}$)**: Composed of the hydrogen cation ($\mathrm{H}^{+}$) and the chloride anion ($\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$).

This is a classic neutralization reaction, which is a specific type of double displacement.

highlights.text: Sodium hydroxide reacts with hydrochloric acid

steps.title: Exchange ion partners
steps.content:
In a double displacement reaction, the cations and anions of the two reactants "swap partners" to form new products:

1.  The **$\mathrm{Na}^{+}$** ion from the first reactant pairs with the **$\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$** ion from the second.
2.  The **$\mathrm{H}^{+}$** ion from the second reactant pairs with the **$\mathrm{OH}^{-}$** ion from the first.

steps.title: Determine product formulas
steps.content:
Now we determine the chemical formulas of the products by ensuring the charges are balanced:

*   **Sodium and Chloride**: $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ($+1$) and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ($-1$) combine in a $1:1$ ratio to form **$\mathrm{NaCl}$**.
*   **Hydrogen and Hydroxide**: $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ ($+1$) and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ ($-1$) combine in a $1:1$ ratio to form $\mathrm{HOH}$, commonly written as **$\mathrm{H_{2}O}$**.

Since all ions have a magnitude of $1$, the formulas are straightforward.

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
Finally, we combine the reactants and products into a chemical equation and verify that it is balanced. 

$$\mathrm{NaOH}(aq) + \mathrm{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}(aq) + \mathrm{H_{2}O}(l)$$

Checking the atom count:
*   $\mathrm{Na}$: $1$ on left, $1$ on right.
*   $\mathrm{Cl}$: $1$ on left, $1$ on right.
*   $\mathrm{H}$: $2$ on left ($1$ from $\mathrm{NaOH}$, $1$ from $\mathrm{HCl}$), $2$ on right (from $\mathrm{H_{2}O}$).
*   $\mathrm{O}$: $1$ on left, $1$ on right.

The equation is perfectly balanced with $1:1$ stoichiometry.

parts.content: Calculate the concentration of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ if $25.0\ \mathrm{cm^3}$ reacts with $25.0\ \mathrm{cm^3}$ of $0.100\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}}$ $\mathrm{HCl}$.
parts.markscheme:
$n(\mathrm{HCl}) = 0.100\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}} \times \frac{25.0}{1000}\ \mathrm{dm^3} = 0.00250\ \mathrm{mol}$ <marks>[1]</marks>
Using 1:1 ratio,
$c(\mathrm{NaOH}) = \frac{0.00250\ \mathrm{mol}}{0.0250\ \mathrm{dm^3}} = 0.100\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}}$ <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Convert volume to dm³
steps.content:
In chemistry calculations, concentration is usually given in $\mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}}$. Therefore, we must convert the volumes from $\mathrm{cm^3}$ to $\mathrm{dm^3}$ by dividing by $1000$.

$$\text{Volume in } \mathrm{dm^3} = \frac{25.0\ \mathrm{cm^3}}{1000} = 0.0250\ \mathrm{dm^3}$$

This conversion is a fundamental step covered in **Topic S1.4: The mole**.

steps.title: Calculate moles of HCl
steps.content:
Using the concentration and volume of hydrochloric acid, we can calculate the amount in moles ($n$) using the formula from your data booklet:

$$n = CV$$

Substituting the values:
$$n(\mathrm{HCl}) = 0.100\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}} \times 0.0250\ \mathrm{dm^3} = 0.00250\ \mathrm{mol}$$

You earn **1 mark** for correctly calculating this value.

gdc.instructions: Multiply 0.100 by (25.0 / 1000) on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the concentration, press multiply, and then enter the volume in dm³.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of HCl by multiplying concentration and volume.

steps.title: Apply stoichiometric ratio
steps.content:
The neutralization reaction between sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid is:
$$\mathrm{NaOH(aq) + HCl(aq) \rightarrow NaCl(aq) + H_2O(l)}$$

From the balanced equation, we see a **1:1 molar ratio**. This means $1\ \text{mole}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$ reacts with exactly $1\ \text{mole}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}$.

$$n(\mathrm{NaOH}) = n(\mathrm{HCl}) = 0.00250\ \mathrm{mol}$$

steps.title: Determine NaOH concentration
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the concentration of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ using the moles we found and its given volume ($25.0\ \mathrm{cm^3}$ or $0.0250\ \mathrm{dm^3}$):

$$\begin{aligned} C(\mathrm{NaOH}) &= \frac{n}{V} \\ &= \frac{0.00250\ \mathrm{mol}}{0.0250\ \mathrm{dm^3}} \\ &= 0.100\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}} \end{aligned}$$

This final step earns the **second mark**.

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.0025 divided by 0.025 on the home screen.

ti84.instructions: Divide the calculated moles (0.0025) by the volume in dm³ (0.025).

calculator.description: Calculate the final concentration by dividing moles by volume.

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
To solve this using the titration formula, we first need to determine the mole ratio from the balanced chemical equation for the neutralization reaction between sodium hydroxide ($\text{NaOH}$) and hydrochloric acid ($\text{HCl}$):

$$\text{NaOH(aq)} + \text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{NaCl(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

From the equation, we can see that 1 mole of $\text{NaOH}$ reacts with 1 mole of $\text{HCl}$. This means our mole ratio is $1:1$. This is a fundamental part of **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

steps.title: Identify the titration formula
steps.content:
We can use the general titration formula, which is derived from the relationship between moles, concentration, and volume ($$n=CV$$) found in your data booklet:

$$\frac{C_1 V_1}{n_1} = \frac{C_2 V_2}{n_2}$$

Where:
- $C_1$ and $V_1$ are the concentration and volume of the $\text{NaOH}$.
- $C_2$ and $V_2$ are the concentration and volume of the $\text{HCl}$.
- $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the coefficients (mole numbers) from the balanced equation (both are $1$ in this case).

steps.title: Substitute the known values
steps.content:
Now, let's plug in the values provided in the question. Since volumes appear on both sides of the equation, we can keep them in $\text{cm}^3$ as the units will cancel out.

$$\begin{aligned} V_1 (\text{NaOH}) &= 25.0 \text{ cm}^3 \\ C_2 (\text{HCl}) &= 0.100 \text{ mol dm}^{-3} \\ V_2 (\text{HCl}) &= 25.0 \text{ cm}^3 \\ n_1 = 1, & \text{ } n_2 = 1 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting these into our formula:

$$\frac{C_{\text{NaOH}} \times 25.0}{1} = \frac{0.100 \times 25.0}{1}$$

highlights.text: 25.0 cm^3 reacts with 25.0 cm^3 of 0.100 mol dm^{-3} HCl

steps.title: Solve for NaOH concentration
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to solve for $C_{\text{NaOH}}$:

$$C_{\text{NaOH}} = \frac{0.100 \times 25.0}{25.0}$$

By calculating this, we find:

$$C_{\text{NaOH}} = 0.100 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$$

This confirms that since the volumes and mole ratios are identical, the concentrations must also be identical to reach the equivalence point.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the concentration of NaOH.

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
First, we look at the neutralization reaction between sodium hydroxide ($\mathrm{NaOH}$) and hydrochloric acid ($\mathrm{HCl}$):

$$\mathrm{NaOH(aq) + HCl(aq) \rightarrow NaCl(aq) + H_2O(l)}$$

From the coefficients in the equation, we can see that the **stoichiometric ratio** between $\mathrm{NaOH}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$ is $1:1$. This means that 1 mole of acid reacts exactly with 1 mole of base. This concept is covered in **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

steps.title: Compare volumes and ratios
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the data provided in the question:
- Volume of $\mathrm{HCl} = 25.0\ \mathrm{cm^3}$
- Volume of $\mathrm{NaOH} = 25.0\ \mathrm{cm^3}$

Notice that the volumes of the acid and the base are **identical**. 

We know from the Data Booklet that the number of moles ($n$) is related to concentration ($C$) and volume ($V$):

$$n = CV$$

Since the reaction ratio is $1:1$, we know that at the neutralization point, the number of moles of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ must equal the number of moles of $\mathrm{HCl}$ ($n_{\mathrm{NaOH}} = n_{\mathrm{HCl}}$).

steps.title: Apply logical proportionality
steps.content:
If the number of moles are equal ($n_1 = n_2$) and the volumes are equal ($V_1 = V_2$), then logically, the concentrations must also be equal ($C_1 = C_2$).

$$\begin{aligned} n_{\mathrm{NaOH}} &= n_{\mathrm{HCl}} \\ C_{\mathrm{NaOH}} \times V_{\mathrm{NaOH}} &= C_{\mathrm{HCl}} \times V_{\mathrm{HCl}} \end{aligned}$$

Since $V_{\mathrm{NaOH}} = V_{\mathrm{HCl}}$, they cancel out in the equation, leaving:

$$C_{\mathrm{NaOH}} = C_{\mathrm{HCl}}$$

Therefore, the concentration of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is the same as the concentration of $\mathrm{HCl}$ given in the question: $0.100\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}}$.

calculator.description: While logic tells us they are equal, we can verify the concentration of NaOH is indeed 0.100 mol/dm³.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
To ensure you get full marks, you should show the value for the moles of $\mathrm{HCl}$ as per the markscheme:

1. Calculate moles of $\mathrm{HCl}$: $$n(\mathrm{HCl}) = 0.100\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}} \times 0.0250\ \mathrm{dm^3} = 0.00250\ \mathrm{mol}$$
2. State the final concentration: $$C(\mathrm{NaOH}) = 0.100\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}}$$

Final Answer: $0.100\ \mathrm{mol\ dm^{-3}}$

parts.content: Determine the amount (mol) of water formed.
parts.markscheme: $n(\mathrm{H_2O}) = 0.00250\ \mathrm{mol}$ <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Identify the balanced equation
steps.content:
To find the amount of water formed, we first need the balanced chemical equation for the neutralization reaction between sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid:

$$\text{NaOH(aq)} + \text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{NaCl(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

This shows us how the reactants and products are related to each other in terms of moles. If you need a refresher on balanced equations, check out **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

steps.title: Determine the molar ratio
steps.content:
The **Stoichiometric Ratio Method** involves looking at the coefficients in front of the formulas in the balanced equation. 

In this reaction, there are no numbers in front of the formulas, which means the coefficients are all 1. Therefore, the molar ratio between the limiting reactant (either $\text{NaOH}$ or $\text{HCl}$) and water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) is $1:1$.

steps.title: Calculate amount of water
steps.content:
Since the ratio is $1:1$, the number of moles of water produced will be exactly the same as the number of moles of the limiting reactant used.

From the data provided in the previous parts of the question, the amount of reactant used was $0.00250\text{ mol}$. Applying the $1:1$ ratio:

$$n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = n(\text{limiting reactant}) = 0.00250\text{ mol}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the moles of limiting reactant by 1.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the moles of limiting reactant by 1.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the moles of limiting reactant by 1.

parts.content:
A gaseous hydrocarbon is found to have an empirical formula of $CH_2$. At standard temperature and pressure (STP), the density of this gas is $1.85\text{ g dm}^{-3}$.

Calculate the molar mass of the hydrocarbon and determine its molecular formula, using $22.7\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1}$ as the molar volume of an ideal gas at STP.

Molar mass:

Molecular formula:
parts.markscheme:
Molar mass:
$M = \text{density} \times \text{molar volume} = 1.85\text{ g dm}^{-3} \times 22.7\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1} = 42.0\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Molecular formula:
$M_r(CH_2) = 12.01 + (2 \times 1.01) = 14.03$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$n = \frac{42.0}{14.03} = 3 \rightarrow C_3H_6$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Silver metal can be recovered from a solution of silver nitrate by a displacement reaction with copper.

$$Cu(s) + 2AgNO_3(aq) \rightarrow Cu(NO_3)_2(aq) + 2Ag(s)$$

A $1.27\text{ g}$ sample of copper is added to $50.0\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.400\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ silver nitrate solution. Determine the limiting reactant and calculate the maximum mass of silver, in grams, that can be produced.
parts.markscheme:
$n(Cu) = \frac{1.27\text{ g}}{63.55\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0200\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$n(AgNO_3) = 0.0500\text{ dm}^3 \times 0.400\text{ mol dm}^{-3} = 0.0200\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Limiting reactant:
According to the ratio ($1:2$), $0.0200\text{ mol}$ of $Cu$ requires $0.0400\text{ mol}$ of $AgNO_3$. Since only $0.0200\text{ mol}$ is available, $AgNO_3$ is the limiting reactant. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Mass of $Ag$:
$n(Ag) = n(AgNO_3) = 0.0200\text{ mol}$
$\text{Mass } Ag = 0.0200\text{ mol} \times 107.87\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 2.16\text{ g}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept $2.15\text{ g}$ if slightly different $A_r$ values are used.</note>

questions.specification:
Nitrogen monoxide gas reacts with oxygen gas to produce nitrogen dioxide gas as shown in the balanced equation below:

$$2\text{NO(g)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2\text{(g)}$$

A chemist mixes the following volumes of the two reactants in a sealed container:

| Reactant gas | Initial volume / $\text{cm}^3$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Nitrogen monoxide ($\text{NO}$) | $40.0$ |
| Oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) | $30.0$ |

Assuming the reaction goes to completion, what is the total volume of gas remaining in the container? (All volumes are measured at the same temperature and pressure.)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.1 How much? The amount of chemical change > R2.1.3 Limiting and excess reactants
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.1.3 Limiting and excess reactants
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses gas volumes with a balanced equation to identify the limiting reactant (NO vs O2) and determine what remains after completion.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.1 How much? The amount of chemical change > R2.1.2 Using the mole ratio
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.1.2 Using the mole ratio
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Relies on stoichiometric mole/volume ratios (2 volumes NO react with 1 volume O2; 2 volumes NO2 produced) to calculate remaining total gas volume.

options.markscheme:
According to Avogadro’s Law, the volume ratio of reacting gases is the same as the mole ratio in the balanced equation.

1. **Determine the limiting reactant:**
From the equation, $2$ volumes of NO react with $1$ volume of $\text{O}_2$.
- $40.0\text{ cm}^3$ of $\text{NO}$ would require $\frac{40.0}{2} = 20.0\text{ cm}^3$ of $\text{O}_2$.
- Since $30.0\text{ cm}^3$ of $\text{O}_2$ is available, $\text{NO}$ is the limiting reactant and $\text{O}_2$ is in excess.

2. **Calculate the volume of product formed:**
The stoichiometric ratio of $\text{NO} : \text{NO}_2$ is $2 : 2$ (or $1 : 1$).
- $40.0\text{ cm}^3$ of $\text{NO}$ produces $40.0\text{ cm}^3$ of $\text{NO}_2$ gas.

3. **Calculate the volume of unreacted excess gas:**
- Remaining $\text{O}_2 = 30.0\text{ cm}^3 - 20.0\text{ cm}^3 = 10.0\text{ cm}^3$.

4. **Calculate the total final volume:**
- Total volume $= V(\text{NO}_2) + V(\text{excess }\text{O}_2)$
- Total volume $= 40.0\text{ cm}^3 + 10.0\text{ cm}^3 = 50.0\text{ cm}^3$.

questions.specification:
Anna wants to determine the empirical formula of magnesium oxide ($\text{MgO}$). Her method involves reacting magnesium ribbon with oxygen from the air in a crucible. The steps of her procedure are:

- Weigh a known mass of magnesium ribbon in a crucible with a lid.
- Heat the crucible using a Bunsen burner until the magnesium reacts completely.
- Cool the crucible and measure the final mass of the product formed.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.4 Counting particles by mass: The mole > S1.4.4 Empirical and molecular formulas
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.4 Empirical and molecular formulas
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The whole practical is about determining an empirical formula from mass changes (Mg to MgO), including ensuring constant mass and discussing sources of error affecting the empirical formula.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b)-(e) focus on experimental design/practical technique (side reactions, lid usage, heating to constant mass, improving accuracy), which links to practical considerations in measuring chemical change during a reaction.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.2 Electron transfer reactions > R3.2.1 Oxidation and reduction definitions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.2.1 Oxidation and reduction definitions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The reaction of magnesium with oxygen is a redox process (Mg oxidized, O2 reduced); understanding competing reactions (e.g., Mg reacting with N2) also relies on redox reasoning.

parts.content: Anna assumes that the only reaction taking place is between magnesium and oxygen from the air. Outline one reason why this assumption might lead to an incorrect empirical formula for the product.
parts.markscheme:
Air contains nitrogen which also reacts with magnesium to form magnesium nitride / $\text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2$. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the composition of air
steps.content: In this experiment, Anna is reacting magnesium with "oxygen from the air". However, it is important to remember from **Topic S1.1.1 (Elements, Compounds and Mixtures)** that air is a mixture. While it contains oxygen ($≈ 21\%$), it is predominantly composed of nitrogen ($≈ 78\%$).

steps.title: Identify the side reaction
steps.content:
At the high temperatures reached using a Bunsen burner, magnesium is reactive enough to react directly with the nitrogen in the air. This side reaction produces magnesium nitride according to the following equation:

$$3\text{Mg(s)} + \text{N}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2\text{(s)}$$

steps.title: Explain the impact
steps.content:
Because some of the magnesium is converted into $\text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2$ instead of $\text{MgO}$, the final mass measured is the mass of a mixture of products. This leads to an incorrect calculation of the mass of oxygen that reacted, ultimately resulting in an inaccurate empirical formula.

To earn the mark, you must mention that **nitrogen reacts with magnesium** to form **magnesium nitride** (or $\text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2$).

parts.content: During the heating process, the crucible lid is periodically lifted slightly and then replaced. Suggest two reasons why this technique is used.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each valid reason, up to <marks>2 marks</marks>:

- Lifting the lid allows air/oxygen to enter the crucible so the reaction can continue to completion. <marks>A1</marks>
- Replacing the lid prevents the loss of the product (magnesium oxide) as white smoke/fine solid particles. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the reaction requirements
steps.content:
To determine the empirical formula of magnesium oxide, we need to ensure that **all** of the magnesium ribbon reacts with oxygen. The chemical equation for this reaction is:

$$2\text{Mg(s)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow 2\text{MgO(s)}$$

Since the reaction takes place inside a closed crucible, the initial amount of oxygen available is limited to the air trapped inside. This leads us to the first reason for moving the lid.

steps.title: Analyze lifting the lid
steps.content:
The first reason for periodically lifting the lid is to allow oxygen from the surrounding air to enter the crucible. 

Without a fresh supply of oxygen, the reaction would stop before all the magnesium is converted to magnesium oxide. Providing extra oxygen ensures the reaction goes to **completion**, which is essential for calculating an accurate empirical formula.

highlights.text: Lifting the lid allows air/oxygen to enter the crucible so the reaction can continue to completion.

steps.title: Analyze replacing the lid
steps.content:
The second reason involves the physical behavior of the product. Magnesium reacts very vigorously (exothermically) with oxygen, producing magnesium oxide in the form of a very fine white powder.

During the reaction, this powder can form a "white smoke." If the lid is left off, these fine solid particles will escape into the air. Replacing the lid quickly ensures that no product is lost, as losing mass would lead to an incorrect mass ratio and a faulty empirical formula.

highlights.text: Replacing the lid prevents the loss of the product (magnesium oxide) as white smoke/fine solid particles.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
In summary, the two reasons for this technique are:
1. To allow **oxygen/air** to enter so the reaction reaches completion.
2. To prevent the **loss of product** (magnesium oxide) as white smoke/particles.

parts.content: Explain why it is important to heat the magnesium until no further change in mass is observed.
parts.markscheme:
- Heating until no further change in mass (heating to constant mass) ensures that the reaction is complete and all magnesium has reacted. <marks>M1</marks>
- This ensures that the final mass of the product is accurate for the calculation of the empirical formula. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the reaction goal
steps.content:
The purpose of this experiment is to determine the empirical formula of magnesium oxide ($\text{MgO}$). This involves measuring how much oxygen ($O_2$) reacts with a specific mass of magnesium ($Mg$) to form the product magnesium oxide ($MgO$).

This falls under **Structure 1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**, where we learn that an empirical formula represents the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound.

highlights.text: determine the empirical formula of magnesium oxide

steps.title: Explain reaction completion
steps.content:
In the laboratory, "heating to a constant mass" is a technique used to ensure a reaction has gone to completion. 

During this reaction, the mass increases because oxygen from the air is combining with the solid magnesium. If the mass is still changing between measurements, it indicates that some magnesium ribbon remains unreacted. Heating until the mass no longer changes ensures that **all the magnesium has been converted into magnesium oxide**.

highlights.text: Heat the crucible using a Bunsen burner until the magnesium reacts completely.

steps.title: Link to accurate calculations
steps.content:
To calculate the empirical formula, we must determine the mass of oxygen reacted using the formula:

$$\text{mass of oxygen} = \text{final mass of product} - \text{initial mass of magnesium}$$

If the reaction is incomplete, the final mass will be lower than it should be. This would result in an incorrect (lower) mass of oxygen, leading to an inaccurate mole ratio and an incorrect empirical formula. Therefore, achieving a constant mass is essential for **data accuracy**.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks, your explanation should include these two points:
1. It ensures the reaction is **complete** (all magnesium has reacted).
2. It ensures the **final mass is accurate** for the calculation of the empirical formula.

parts.content: Suggest how Anna's method could be modified to improve the accuracy of determining the empirical formula of magnesium oxide, assuming access to modern laboratory equipment.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any one of the following:

- Use a controlled supply of pure oxygen (instead of air) to ensure complete reaction and prevent nitride formation. <marks>A1</marks>
- Use an analytical balance with higher precision (e.g., $\pm 0.0001\text{ g}$) to reduce the percentage uncertainty in mass measurements. <marks>A1</marks>
- Use a temperature-controlled furnace to ensure the magnesium reaches the required temperature without excessive heating. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the limitation
steps.content: In Anna's current method, she uses **oxygen from the air**. While this is standard for school labs, air is a mixture containing approximately $78\%$ nitrogen and only $21\%$ oxygen. This leads to two main issues: side reactions and potential incompleteness.

steps.title: Prevent magnesium nitride formation
steps.content:
When magnesium is heated in air, it doesn't just react with oxygen; it can also react with nitrogen to form magnesium nitride ($Mg_3N_2$). 

$$\text{3Mg(s)} + \text{N}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2\text{(s)}$$

This side product has a different mass ratio than magnesium oxide ($MgO$), which will cause an error when calculating the empirical formula. By using a **controlled supply of pure oxygen**, we eliminate nitrogen from the system entirely, ensuring only $MgO$ is produced.

steps.title: Ensure complete reaction
steps.content:
Using a pure oxygen supply significantly increases the concentration of the reactant. According to collision theory (which you can review in **Reactivity 2.2**), a higher concentration of oxygen increases the frequency of successful collisions, helping to ensure that every bit of the magnesium ribbon reacts completely.

In the original method, if the reaction is incomplete, the final mass would be too low, leading to an incorrect ratio of magnesium to oxygen.

steps.title: Final recommendation
steps.content: To improve accuracy, Anna should perform the reaction in a closed system connected to a gas cylinder of **pure oxygen**. This modification prevents the formation of nitrides and ensures a 100% yield of magnesium oxide, providing a much more accurate mass of oxygen for the empirical formula calculation.

highlights.text: Use a controlled supply of pure oxygen (instead of air) to ensure complete reaction and prevent nitride formation.

steps.title: Identify mass measurement importance
steps.content:
To determine the empirical formula of magnesium oxide, Anna needs to calculate the moles of magnesium and oxygen. These calculations are based entirely on the **mass** measurements recorded during the experiment. 

Any uncertainty in the balance used directly affects the precision of the final mole ratio. To improve this, we can focus on the equipment used for weighing.

steps.title: Use high-precision analytical balance
steps.content: A significant improvement would be to use an **analytical balance** with a higher level of precision, such as one that measures to four decimal places ($ \pm 0.0001\text{ g} $), instead of a standard laboratory balance (which typically measures to $ \pm 0.01\text{ g} $).

highlights.text: Use an analytical balance with higher precision (e.g., ± 0.0001 g) to reduce the percentage uncertainty in mass measurements.

steps.title: Minimize percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
By using a more precise balance, you drastically reduce the **percentage uncertainty** in your data. 

For example, if you weigh $0.20\text{ g}$ of magnesium:
- With a standard balance ($ \pm 0.01\text{ g} $), the uncertainty is $5\%$.
- With an analytical balance ($ \pm 0.0001\text{ g} $), the uncertainty drops to $0.05\%$.

This high level of precision ensures that the calculated ratio of Mg to O is as close to the true whole-number ratio as possible.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the percentage uncertainties for both balances.

ti84.instructions: Calculate (0.01 / 0.20) * 100 for the standard balance and (0.0001 / 0.20) * 100 for the analytical balance.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the percentage uncertainties for both balances.

calculator.description: Compare percentage uncertainty between a standard balance and a high-precision analytical balance for a 0.20g sample.

steps.title: Analyze the current limitation
steps.content: In the original procedure, Anna uses a Bunsen burner to heat the magnesium. While common in school labs, Bunsen burners provide uneven heating and it is difficult to control the exact temperature. This can lead to "spattering," where some of the product is lost as the reaction becomes too vigorous, or the magnesium may not reach a consistent temperature for a complete reaction.

highlights.text: Heat the crucible using a Bunsen burner

steps.title: Suggest a modern modification
steps.content: To improve the accuracy, Anna could replace the Bunsen burner with a **temperature-controlled furnace** (or oven). This modern piece of equipment allows for much more precise control over the heating environment compared to an open flame.

highlights.text: Use a temperature-controlled furnace to ensure the magnesium reaches the required temperature without excessive heating.

steps.title: Explain the accuracy improvement
steps.content: A furnace provides uniform heating, ensuring that all the magnesium reaches the required temperature to react fully without the risk of "excessive heating." This prevents the crucible from getting too hot, which reduces the chance of the product spattering out or the magnesium vaporizing, both of which would cause a loss of mass and result in an incorrect empirical formula for $\text{MgO}$.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The suggested modification is to use a **temperature-controlled furnace** to ensure the magnesium reaches the required reaction temperature without excessive heating or loss of product through spattering.

What is the percentage uncertainty in the volume of solution delivered?

parts.content:
| Reactant | Initial amount / mol |
| :--- | :--- |
| Aluminum ($$Al$$) | $$0.500$$ |
| Chlorine ($$Cl_2$$) | $$0.600$$ |

Using the balanced equation $$2	\text{Al(s)} + 3	\text{Cl}_2	ext{(g)} 
\rightarrow 2	\text{AlCl}_3	\text{(s)}$$, deduce the maximum amount, in mol, of aluminum chloride that can be produced from this mixture.
parts.markscheme:
- Identification of chlorine as limiting and calculation of product: $$n(	\text{AlCl}_3) = \frac{2}{3} 	\times 0.600 = 0.400 	\text{ mol}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award the mark for the correct numerical value with units. No credit for $$0.500 	\text{ mol}$$ (based on Al).</note>

parts.content: Determine the mass, in g, of the reactant that remains in excess at the end of the reaction. ($$A_r(Al) = 26.98$$)
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the moles of aluminum reacted: $$n(	\text{Al reacted}) = \frac{2}{3} 	\times 0.600 = 0.400 	\text{ mol}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate the mass of unreacted aluminum: $$(0.500 - 0.400) 	\times 26.98 = 2.70 	\text{ g}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept $$2.7 	\text{ g}$$. Apply ECF from part (a) if the student identified aluminum as the limiting reactant.</note>
<examTip>Always check which reactant is in excess before calculating the final mass of remaining material.</examTip>

questions.specification:
Thomas wants to determine the empirical formula of red-brown copper oxide. The method he chooses is to convert a known amount of copper(II) sulfate into this oxide. The steps of his procedure are:
- Make $100 \mathrm{\ cm}^3$ of a $1 \mathrm{\ mol\ dm}^{-3}$ solution using hydrated copper(II) sulfate crystals.
- React a known volume of this solution with alkaline glucose in order to convert it to red-brown copper oxide.
- Separate the precipitated oxide and find its mass.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.4 Counting particles by mass: The mole > S1.4.5 Molar concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.5 Molar concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Preparing a 1 mol dm^-3 solution in 100 cm^3 and discussion of volumetric flask vs measuring cylinder focuses on accurate solution preparation and concentration/volume measurement.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.4 Counting particles by mass: The mole > S1.4.2 Relative atomic and formula masses
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.2 Relative atomic and formula masses
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) involves calculating a required mass from a formula mass; the major error is neglecting water of crystallization in hydrated CuSO4, i.e., incorrect formula mass.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.4 Counting particles by mass: The mole > S1.4.3 Molar mass
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.3 Molar mass
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Related to converting between moles and mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate crystals using molar mass (including waters of hydration).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.1 How much? The amount of chemical change > R2.1.2 Using the mole ratio
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.1.2 Using the mole ratio
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Converting a known volume of CuSO4 solution to a mass of copper oxide requires applying mole ratios between reactant and product (stoichiometric relationships).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.4 Counting particles by mass: The mole > S1.4.4 Empirical and molecular formulas
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.4 Empirical and molecular formulas
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The overall aim is determining an empirical formula of copper oxide from measured masses, which aligns with empirical formula determination.

parts.content: Thomas calculates that he needs $0.1 \times (1\times 63.55 +1 \times 32.07 + 4 \times 16.00 )=15.962 \pm 0.001 \mathrm{\ g}$ of the copper(II) sulfate to make the solution. Outline the major error in his calculation.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark max </marks> for any of the following:

- forgot to take account of water of crystallization
- should have used 24.972 g

steps.title: Calculate the required moles
steps.content:
To find how much substance Thomas needs, we first calculate the number of moles ($n$) required for the solution. Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = CV$$

Given the volume is $100\text{ cm}^3$ ($0.1\text{ dm}^3$) and the concentration is $1\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$:

$$n = 1\text{ mol dm}^{-3} \times 0.1\text{ dm}^3 = 0.1\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Analyze Thomas's molar mass
steps.content:
Thomas calculates the mass using the formula $m = n \times M$. Let's look at the molar mass ($M$) he used:

$$M = 63.55 + 32.07 + (4 \times 16.00) = 159.62\text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

This calculation only accounts for copper (Cu), sulfur (S), and four oxygen (O) atoms. This corresponds to **anhydrous** copper(II) sulfate ($	ext{CuSO}_4$).

steps.title: Identify the missing component
steps.content:
The procedure specifies that Thomas is using **hydrated copper(II) sulfate crystals**. The common hydrated form is $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$. 

This means for every one mole of $\text{CuSO}_4$, there are also five moles of water trapped in the crystal lattice. This 'water of crystallization' must be included in the molar mass calculation to get the correct mass of the crystals.

steps.title: Identify the major error
steps.content:
Thomas's major error is that he **forgot to take into account the water of crystallization**. 

Because he used the anhydrous molar mass ($159.62\text{ g mol}^{-1}$) instead of the hydrated molar mass ($≈ 249.72\text{ g mol}^{-1}$), he will not weigh out enough copper(II) sulfate to reach the desired $1\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ concentration.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the molar mass of 5 water molecules and add it to the anhydrous mass, then multiply by 0.1.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the molar mass of 5 water molecules and add it to the anhydrous mass, then multiply by 0.1.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the molar mass of 5 water molecules and add it to the anhydrous mass, then multiply by 0.1.

calculator.description: Calculate the correct mass needed for hydrated copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O).

parts.content: He now adds $100 \pm 1 \mathrm{\ cm}^3$ of water from a measuring/graduated cylinder and dissolves the copper(II) sulfate crystals. A friend tells him that for making standard solutions it is better to use a volumetric flask rather than adding water from a measuring cylinder. Suggest two reasons why a volumetric flask is better.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark</marks> for each of the following, <marks> 2 marks max</marks>

- less uncertainty in the volume / more precise
- takes into account volume change on dissolving / concentration is for a given volume of solution not volume of solvent

steps.title: Compare absolute uncertainty
steps.content:
The first reason involves the **physical precision** of the equipment. The question states that the measuring cylinder provides a volume of $100 \pm 1 \text{ cm}^3$. This means the absolute uncertainty is $1 \text{ cm}^3$.

In contrast, a standard $100 \text{ cm}^3$ volumetric flask is manufactured to much tighter tolerances (typically around $\pm 0.08 \text{ cm}^3$). Because the flask has a narrow neck, it is much easier to see exactly when the meniscus reaches the calibration mark, leading to **less uncertainty** and higher precision.

steps.title: Evaluate percentage uncertainty
steps.content: To see the impact of this precision, we can calculate the percentage uncertainty for the measuring cylinder. Let's use the calculator to find the percentage for the cylinder's measurement.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 divided by 100, then multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage uncertainty for the measuring cylinder ($1 \text{ cm}^3$ uncertainty in $100 \text{ cm}^3$).

steps.title: Compare the results
steps.content: The percentage uncertainty for the cylinder is $1\%$. For a volumetric flask (with an uncertainty of roughly $0.08 \text{ cm}^3$), the percentage uncertainty would be approximately $0.08\%$. This confirms that the **volumetric flask is significantly more precise**.

steps.title: Address solution volume definition
steps.content:
The second reason relates to the definition of molar concentration found in **Topic S1.4.5: Molar concentration** and the data booklet:

$$n = CV \implies C = \frac{n}{V}$$

In chemistry, $V$ represents the **total volume of the solution**, not just the volume of the solvent added. When Thomas adds $100 \text{ cm}^3$ of water to crystals, the final volume of the mixture might change slightly as the crystals dissolve. 

A volumetric flask solves this because you dissolve the solute first and then add water until the **total volume of the solution** is exactly $100 \text{ cm}^3$.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, the two suggested reasons why a volumetric flask is better are:

1. It has **less uncertainty** in the volume measurement (it is more precise).
2. It ensures the concentration is calculated for a **given volume of solution** rather than just a volume of solvent (it accounts for volume changes during dissolving).

steps.title: Define Molar Concentration
steps.content:
To understand why a volumetric flask is necessary, we must first look at the definition of molar concentration ($C$). In the IB Data Booklet, we find the relationship between moles ($n$), concentration ($C$), and volume ($V$):

$$n = CV$$

Crucially, in chemistry, the volume ($V$) in this formula refers to the **total volume of the solution**, not just the volume of the solvent (water) added.

steps.title: Solution vs Solvent Volume
steps.content:
When you add $100\text{ cm}^3$ of water to crystals using a measuring cylinder, you are measuring the **solvent volume**. However, as the copper(II) sulfate crystals dissolve, they occupy space within the liquid. 

This means the final total volume of the resulting solution will likely be different from $100\text{ cm}^3$. Since concentration depends on the final solution volume, adding a fixed amount of water results in an inaccurate concentration.

highlights.text: 100 \pm 1 \mathrm{\ cm}^3$ of water from a measuring/graduated cylinder

steps.title: Function of Volumetric Flasks
steps.content:
A volumetric flask is designed to overcome the volume change during dissolving. You typically dissolve the solute in a smaller amount of water first, and then carefully add more water until the bottom of the meniscus reaches the graduation mark. 

This ensures that the **final solution volume** is exactly $100.00\text{ cm}^3$, accounting for any volume changes that occurred during the dissolving process.

steps.title: Precision and Uncertainty
steps.content:
The second reason involves the precision of the glassware. A measuring cylinder is generally a "Class B" instrument with a relatively high uncertainty (e.g., $\pm 1\text{ cm}^3$). 

In contrast, a volumetric flask is a "Class A" instrument designed for high accuracy, offering a much **lower uncertainty** and more precise measurement of the total volume.

highlights.text: less uncertainty in the volume / more precise

parts.content: Thomas now heats $25 \mathrm{\ cm}^3$ of the solution with excess alkaline glucose to convert it to a suspension of red-brown copper oxide. Describe how he can obtain the pure, dry solid product.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 2 marks</marks> for all 3, <marks> 1 mark</marks> for any 2.

- filter / centrifuge
- rinse (the solid) with water
- heat in an oven / rinse with propanone/ethanol/volatile organic solvent and leave to evaporate

steps.title: Separation by Filtration
steps.content:
To start, we need to separate the solid **red-brown copper oxide** from the liquid reaction mixture. This is done using **filtration**. 

You should pour the suspension through filter paper held in a funnel. The solid copper oxide (the residue) will remain on the filter paper, while the liquid (the filtrate) passes through.

steps.title: Rinsing for Purity
steps.content:
To ensure the product is **pure**, you must wash the solid residue while it is still on the filter paper. 

Rinse the solid with **distilled water**. This step is crucial because it removes any remaining soluble impurities, such as excess alkaline glucose or unreacted copper(II) sulfate, which would otherwise add extra mass to your final measurement.

steps.title: Drying in an Oven
steps.content:
Finally, to obtain a **dry solid product**, the moisture must be removed. 

Place the washed solid in an **oven** set at a low temperature (or leave it in a desiccator) until it reaches a constant mass. This ensures all the water used for rinsing has evaporated, leaving only the copper oxide behind for an accurate empirical formula calculation.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn the full **2 marks** in an IB exam, you must mention all three key technical steps:
1. **Filter** the mixture.
2. **Rinse** or wash the solid with water.
3. **Heat** or dry the solid in an oven.

Note: Mentioning only two of these steps would typically earn 1 mark.

steps.title: Separate via centrifugation
steps.content: To separate the solid red-brown copper oxide from the liquid mixture, the suspension should be placed in a centrifuge. The rapid rotation will force the solid precipitate to the bottom of the tube, allowing the liquid (supernatant) to be easily poured off or decanted.

steps.title: Rinse with distilled water
steps.content: Once the initial liquid is removed, the solid should be rinsed with distilled water. This is a critical purification step to wash away any remaining soluble impurities, such as excess alkaline glucose or sulfate ions from the original solution.

steps.title: Wash with volatile solvent
steps.content: After rinsing with water, perform a final wash using a volatile organic solvent such as propanone or ethanol. These solvents are used because they are miscible with water and have much lower boiling points, which aids in the drying process.

steps.title: Dry by rapid evaporation
steps.content: Finally, allow the remaining solvent to evaporate. Because propanone or ethanol is highly volatile, it will evaporate rapidly at room temperature (or with very gentle warming), leaving behind the pure, dry copper oxide solid ready for massing.

parts.content: Using the same chemical reactions, suggest how Thomas’ method to determine the mass of red-brown copper oxide that could be obtained from a known mass of copper(II) sulfate crystals might be simplified to produce more precise results.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark max </marks> for any of the following:
- taking a known mass of the solid to react directly with glucose
- not making the standard solution

steps.title: Analyze current procedure
steps.content:
In Thomas's current method, there are several steps where errors can accumulate:
1. Weighing the hydrated copper(II) sulfate.
2. Transferring it to a volumetric flask to make a $100 \text{ cm}^3$ solution.
3. Measuring out a specific volume (an aliquot) of that solution to react with glucose.

Each transfer and volumetric measurement introduces a small amount of uncertainty.

highlights.text: Make 100 \mathrm{\ cm}^3$ of a $1 \mathrm{\ mol\ dm}^{-3}$ solution

steps.title: Simplify with direct reaction
steps.content: To produce more precise results, we can use a **Direct Solid Reaction**. Instead of making a standard solution, Thomas should weigh a specific mass of the hydrated copper(II) sulfate crystals and add them directly to the alkaline glucose solution.

highlights.text: taking a known mass of the solid to react directly with glucose

steps.title: Explain the benefit
steps.content:
By reacting the solid directly, you eliminate the need for volumetric flasks and pipettes. This reduces:
- **Transfer losses**: No copper ions are left behind in the beaker or flask used to make the solution.
- **Volumetric uncertainty**: You remove the errors associated with filling a flask to the mark or pipetting an aliquot.

This makes the relationship between the starting mass of the reactant and the final mass of the product much more direct and precise.

questions.specification:
Propane, $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{8}(\mathrm{g})$, reacts with oxygen to form carbon dioxide and water according to the following equation:

$$ \mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{8}(\mathrm{g}) + 5\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 3\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) + 4\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) $$

A mixture of $20\,\mathrm{cm}^{3}$ of propane and $120\,\mathrm{cm}^{3}$ of oxygen is ignited. What is the total volume of gas remaining, in $\mathrm{cm}^{3}$, after the reaction is complete? Assume constant temperature and pressure; $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$ condenses.

options.markscheme: If propane were limiting: $20\times 3=60\,\mathrm{cm}^3$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ forms, but leftover $\mathrm{O}_2$ must also be included in the total gas volume.
This corresponds to the $\mathrm{O}_2$ volume required to react with $20\,\mathrm{cm}^3$ propane ($20\times 5=100$), not the total gas remaining.

At same $T,P$, volume $\propto$ moles. $20\,\mathrm{cm}^3$ $\mathrm{C}_3\mathrm{H}_8$ requires $20\times 5=100\,\mathrm{cm}^3$ $\mathrm{O}_2$, so propane is limiting and $\mathrm{O}_2$ left over is $120-100=20\,\mathrm{cm}^3$. Gas produced: $\mathrm{CO}_2=20\times 3=60\,\mathrm{cm}^3$. Total gas remaining $=60+20=80\,\mathrm{cm}^3$.
This incorrectly adds initial gas volumes without accounting for reactant consumption and the fact that $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ is liquid and not counted as gas.
