labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Multiple parts require converting between mass and amount in moles using molar masses (H2O, AgCl, MgCl2).

parts.content: Explain why the sample was heated three times.
parts.markscheme: To ensure all water was driven off / to heat to constant mass. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the experimental goal
steps.content: In this experiment, the student is trying to determine the amount of water in a hydrated salt ($MgCl_2 \cdot xH_2O$). To do this accurately, they must evaporate every single molecule of the water of crystallisation, leaving only the anhydrous salt behind. This topic is covered in **Structure 1.4: Counting particles by mass**.

steps.title: Analyze the mass changes
steps.content:
If we look at the data, the mass decreases significantly after the first heating. However, it continues to decrease (by $0.005\text{ g}$) between the first and second heating. 

$$\text{Mass change} = 23.982\text{ g} - 23.977\text{ g} = 0.005\text{ g}$$

This small change tells us that the dehydration process wasn't quite finished after just one go.

steps.title: Understand constant mass
steps.content: The **Heating to Constant Mass** approach is the standard laboratory procedure to ensure a reaction is complete. By heating the sample a third time and observing that the mass remained exactly $23.977\text{ g}$, the student confirmed that no more water was being lost.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: The sample was heated three times to ensure that all the water of crystallisation was driven off and that the sample had reached a **constant mass**.

steps.title: Analyze the mass changes
steps.content:
Let's look at the data provided for the heating process:

*   **Mass after first heating:** $23.982\text{ g}$
*   **Mass after second heating:** $23.977\text{ g}$
*   **Mass after third heating:** $23.977\text{ g}$

Notice that the mass decreased between the first and second heating. This indicates that there was still some water of crystallisation remaining in the $MgCl_2 \cdot xH_2O$ sample after the first round of heating.

steps.title: Heating to constant mass
steps.content:
In chemistry, we use a technique called **heating to constant mass**. This involves heating the sample, cooling it, weighing it, and then repeating the process until the mass no longer changes. 

Since the mass remained identical ($23.977\text{ g}$) between the second and third heatings, the student has confirmed that no more water is being lost.

steps.title: Ensuring complete dehydration
steps.content:
The fundamental goal of this experiment is **complete dehydration**. If any water remained in the sample, the calculated mass of the anhydrous salt would be too high, which would lead to an incorrect (lower) value for $x$ in the formula $MgCl_2 \cdot xH_2O$.

Heating three times ensures that all water of crystallisation has been driven off, leaving behind only the pure anhydrous magnesium chloride.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you should state that the repeated heating is necessary to ensure all water has been removed or to reach a constant mass. 

This is a standard practical consideration in **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**, where accurate mass measurements are essential for determining empirical formulas.

parts.content: Use the data to calculate the mass of water and the amount (in mol) lost in the experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Mass of water lost: $25.825 - 23.977 = 1.848 \text{ g}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Amount (in mol): $1.848 / 18.02 = 0.1026 \text{ mol}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the constant mass
steps.content:
To find out how much water was lost, we first need to determine the final mass of the container and the dry sample. In chemistry experiments, we heat a sample until the mass no longer changes, which indicates all the water has been driven off. 

Looking at the data, the mass became constant after the second and third heatings.

steps.title: Calculate mass of water
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Mass Subtraction** approach, we subtract the final constant mass from the initial mass of the hydrated sample and container. This difference represents the mass of the water that evaporated.

$$\text{Mass of water} = \text{Initial mass} - \text{Final constant mass}$$
$$\text{Mass of water} = 25.825\text{ g} - 23.977\text{ g} = 1.848\text{ g}$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract 23.977 from 25.825 in the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the initial mass, then the minus sign, followed by the final mass.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Subtraction of the final mass from the initial mass.

highlights.text: Initial mass of sample and container

steps.title: Calculate amount in moles
steps.content:
Now we convert the mass of water into moles ($n$) using its molar mass ($M = 18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$). This relates to **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**, where we use the formula found in the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\text{Moles of water} = \frac{1.848\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.1026\text{ mol}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the calculated mass of water (1.848) by the molar mass of water (18.02).

calculator.description: Division of mass by molar mass to find moles.

steps.title: Identify the constant mass
steps.content:
In experiments like this, we heat the sample until the mass no longer changes. This ensures all water has been driven off. Looking at the data, the mass became constant after the second and third heatings.

$$ \text{Final constant mass (residue + container)} = 23.977 \text{ g} $$

steps.title: Isolate the component masses
steps.content:
First, we calculate the mass of the hydrated salt (before heating) and the mass of the anhydrous residue (after heating) by subtracting the mass of the empty container from the totals.

**Mass of hydrated salt:**
$$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{hydrated}} &= \text{Initial mass} - \text{Mass of empty container} \\ m_{\text{hydrated}} &= 25.825 \text{ g} - 22.347 \text{ g} \\ m_{\text{hydrated}} &= 3.478 \text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

**Mass of anhydrous residue:**
$$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{anhydrous}} &= \text{Final mass} - \text{Mass of empty container} \\ m_{\text{anhydrous}} &= 23.977 \text{ g} - 22.347 \text{ g} \\ m_{\text{anhydrous}} &= 1.630 \text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: Initial mass of sample and container

steps.title: Calculate mass of water lost
steps.content:
The mass of the water lost is simply the difference between the hydrated salt and the anhydrous residue.

$$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{water}} &= m_{\text{hydrated}} - m_{\text{anhydrous}} \\ m_{\text{water}} &= 3.478 \text{ g} - 1.630 \text{ g} \\ m_{\text{water}} &= 1.848 \text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

*Note: You could also calculate this directly by subtracting the final mass from the initial mass ($25.825 - 23.977 = 1.848 \text{ g}$), which would earn you the first mark in the exam.*

steps.title: Convert mass to moles
steps.content:
Now we convert the mass of water to an amount in moles. We use the molar mass of water ($H_2O$), which is approximately $18.02 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

Referring to the **Key Chemistry Equations** in your data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} n_{\text{water}} &= \frac{1.848 \text{ g}}{18.02 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n_{\text{water}} &= 0.1026 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

This provides the second mark. The final answer is **0.1026 mol**.

ti84.instructions: In the main calculation screen, divide 1.848 by 18.02.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles by dividing the mass of water by its molar mass.

parts.content: Use your answer to Part 2 to determine the formula of the hydrated compound.
parts.markscheme:
Mass of anhydrous $MgCl_2 = 23.977 - 22.347 = 1.630 \text{ g}$

Amount (in mol) $= 1.630 / 95.20 = 0.01712 \text{ mol}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Ratio of $H_2O$ to $MgCl_2 = 0.1026 / 0.01712 = 5.993 \approx 6$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Empirical formula is: $MgCl_2 \cdot 6H_2O$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Calculate anhydrous salt mass
steps.content:
To find the mass of the dry $MgCl_2$ remaining after heating, subtract the mass of the empty container from the final constant mass after the third heating.

$$\text{Mass of } MgCl_2 = 23.977\text{ g} - 22.347\text{ g} = 1.630\text{ g}$$

calculator.description: Subtract the container mass from the final mass.

steps.title: Calculate moles of MgCl2
steps.content:
Using the molar mass of $MgCl_2$ ($95.21\text{ g mol}^{-1}$), we calculate the amount in moles using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$n(MgCl_2) = \frac{1.630\text{ g}}{95.21\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.01712\text{ mol}$$

calculator.description: Divide the mass by the molar mass.

steps.title: Calculate moles of water
steps.content:
From Part 2, we found the amount of water lost was $0.1026\text{ mol}$. 

This was calculated by taking the mass lost during heating ($25.825\text{ g} - 23.977\text{ g} = 1.848\text{ g}$) and dividing by the molar mass of water ($18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$):

$$n(H_2O) = \frac{1.848\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.1026\text{ mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate moles of water from mass lost.

steps.title: Determine the mole ratio
steps.content:
The value of $x$ represents the ratio of moles of $H_2O$ to moles of $MgCl_2$. 

$$x = \frac{n(H_2O)}{n(MgCl_2)} = \frac{0.1026\text{ mol}}{0.01712\text{ mol}} \approx 5.993$$

Rounding to the nearest whole number, we get $x = 6$.

calculator.description: Divide moles of water by moles of salt.

steps.title: State the final formula
steps.content:
By substituting the calculated value of $x$ into the hydrated salt formula, we find the formula for the hydrated compound.

$$\text{Formula: } MgCl_2 \cdot 6H_2O$$

steps.title: Calculate masses from data
steps.content:
First, we need to find the mass of the water lost and the total mass of the initial hydrated sample from the experimental data. 

The mass of water lost is the difference between the initial sample mass and the final constant mass:
$$\text{Mass of water} = 25.825\text{ g} - 23.977\text{ g} = 1.848\text{ g}$$

The total mass of the hydrated sample is the difference between the initial mass of the sample + container and the empty container:
$$\text{Mass of hydrate} = 25.825\text{ g} - 22.347\text{ g} = 3.478\text{ g}$$

highlights.text: Initial mass of sample and container

steps.title: Find mass percentage of water
steps.content:
Using the **Mass Percentage Method**, we calculate the percentage of water by mass in our experimental sample:

$$\text{Mass \% } \text{H}_2\text{O} = \frac{\text{mass of water}}{\text{total mass of hydrated sample}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Mass \% } \text{H}_2\text{O} = \frac{1.848\text{ g}}{3.478\text{ g}} \times 100 \approx 53.13\%$$

gdc.instructions: Type 1.848 / 3.478 into the main calculation screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass percentage of water.

steps.title: Set up theoretical relationship
steps.content:
The theoretical mass percentage is based on the molar masses of $MgCl_2$ and $H_2O$. From the Periodic Table (as used in **S1.4 Counting particles by mass**):
- $M(MgCl_2) = 24.31 + (2 \times 35.45) = 95.21\text{ g mol}^{-1}$
- $M(H_2O) = (2 \times 1.01) + 16.00 = 18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$

The theoretical mass percentage is:
$$\text{Mass \% } \text{H}_2\text{O} = \frac{x \times M(\text{H}_2\text{O})}{M(MgCl_2) + (x \times M(\text{H}_2\text{O}))}$$

Substituting our experimental percentage (as a decimal) and the molar masses:
$$0.5313 = \frac{18.02x}{95.21 + 18.02x}$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Now we solve the algebraic equation for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.5313(95.21 + 18.02x) &= 18.02x \\ 50.589 + 9.574x &= 18.02x \\ 50.589 &= 8.446x \\ x &\approx 5.99 \end{aligned}$$

Since $x$ must be a whole number in a chemical formula, we round to **6**.

gdc.instructions: Use the Equation Solver or Plot f(x) = (18.02*x)/(95.21 + 18.02*x) and find where y = 0.5313.

ti84.instructions: Use Solver (MATH -> B:Solver). Enter 0 = (18.02*X)/(95.21 + 18.02*X) - 0.5313 and solve for X.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.5313 = (18.02x) / (95.21 + 18.02x) and look for the x-intercept.

calculator.description: Solve the equation for x.

steps.title: State the final formula
steps.content:
The value of $x$ is 6, which represents the number of water molecules of crystallisation. 

The formula of the hydrated compound is:
$$\text{MgCl}_2 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Calculate experimental component masses
steps.content:
First, we determine the mass of the dry (anhydrous) salt and the water that was evaporated. We use the constant mass from the third heating ($23.977\text{ g}$) as our final value to ensure all water is gone.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Mass of } MgCl_2 &= 23.977\text{ g} - 22.347\text{ g} = 1.630\text{ g} \\ \text{Mass of } H_2O &= 25.825\text{ g} - 23.977\text{ g} = 1.848\text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Scale water to one mole
steps.content:
Using the **Mass Scaling Method**, we calculate the mass of water that would be present if we had exactly one mole of $MgCl_2$ ($95.20\text{ g/mol}$). 

We multiply the experimental mass ratio by the molar mass of the salt:

$$\text{Mass of water per mole of salt} = \left( \frac{1.848\text{ g } H_2O}{1.630\text{ g } MgCl_2} \right) \times 95.20\text{ g/mol}$$

$$\text{Mass of water per mole of salt} \approx 107.93\text{ g/mol}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the mass ratio scaled to the molar mass of the salt.

ti84.instructions: Enter the division of masses followed by multiplication by the molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of water associated with one mole of anhydrous salt.

steps.title: Determine the value of x
steps.content:
Now, we convert this scaled mass of water into moles by dividing by the molar mass of water ($18.02\text{ g/mol}$). This uses the standard mole formula:

$$\text{data\_booklet: } n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\begin{aligned} x &= \frac{107.93\text{ g/mol}}{18.02\text{ g/mol}} \\ x &\approx 5.99 \end{aligned}$$

Since $x$ must be an integer in a chemical formula, we round to $x = 6$.

calculator.description: Divide the scaled mass by the molar mass of water to find x.

steps.title: State final hydrated formula
steps.content:
By finding that $x = 6$, we can write the complete formula for the hydrated compound.

The formula is **$MgCl_2 \cdot 6H_2O$**.

According to the markscheme, you earn marks for calculating the amount of salt, identifying the $6:1$ ratio, and stating the final formula.

parts.content: Predict the effect of some of the solid being spilt from the crucible on the mass of water lost.
parts.markscheme: The calculated mass of water lost will be too large. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Define the calculation formula
steps.content:
To find the mass of water lost from a hydrated salt, we use a basic subtraction formula. We assume that any change in mass is solely due to the water of crystallisation evaporating away:

$$\text{Mass of water lost} = \text{Initial mass (sample + container)} - \text{Final mass (residue + container)}$$

steps.title: Identify the error source
steps.content:
In this scenario, some of the solid is spilt from the crucible. This means the balance will record a **lower** final mass than if only the water had been lost. 

The calculation logic still "believes" that the total mass lost (whether it was water or spilt solid) is entirely composed of water.

highlights.text: solid being spilt from the crucible

steps.title: Analyze the formula logic
steps.content:
Let's look at how a lower final mass affects our subtraction:

$$\text{Mass of water lost} = \text{Initial mass} - \downarrow \text{Final mass}$$

When you subtract a smaller number from the initial mass, the resulting difference becomes **larger**. Mathematically, as the subtrahend decreases, the result (the difference) increases.

steps.title: State the final prediction
steps.content:
Because the calculation treats the loss of solid as if it were additional water escaping, the final result will be over-estimated.

Therefore, the calculated mass of water lost will be **too large**.

gdc.instructions:
1. Enter the original subtraction: 25.825 - 23.977.
2. Enter a hypothetical spillage subtraction: 25.825 - 23.000.
3. Observe that the result increases when the final mass decreases.

ti84.instructions:
1. Calculate original water mass: 25.825 - 23.977.
2. Calculate with spillage (assume final mass drops to 23.000): 25.825 - 23.000.
3. Compare the results.

desmos.instructions: Enter the original mass loss calculation: 25.825 - 23.977. Then enter a version with a lower final mass (spillage): 25.825 - 23.000.

calculator.description: Compare the original calculation with a hypothetical spillage case.

steps.title: Identify the experimental assumption
steps.content:
In this experiment, the fundamental assumption is that the decrease in mass during heating is due **entirely** to the evaporation of the water of crystallization. 

Mathematically, the student calculates the mass of water lost using:
$$\text{mass of water lost} = \text{initial mass of sample and container} - \text{final mass after heating}$$

steps.title: Analyze the effect of spilling
steps.content:
If some of the solid $MgCl_2$ is spilt from the crucible during the process, the **final mass after heating** will be lower than it should be. 

This is because the mass is being lost in two ways:
1. Evaporation of water (intended)
2. Physical loss of the solid sample (unintended)

highlights.text: solid being spilt from the crucible

steps.title: Evaluate the calculated result
steps.content:
Because the student assumes all mass loss is water, the physical loss of the spilt solid is incorrectly attributed to water evaporation. 

Since the "Final mass" in our equation is smaller than expected, the calculated difference (the "mass of water lost") will be **larger** than the actual amount of water that was present in the sample.

steps.title: Final Prediction
steps.content: Therefore, the calculated mass of water lost will be **too large** (or overestimated).

highlights.text: The calculated mass of water lost will be too large.

steps.title: Define the calculation
steps.content:
To determine the mass of water lost, we subtract the final mass of the container (after all water has been driven off) from the initial mass of the sample and container.

$$\text{Mass of water lost} = \text{Initial mass} - \text{Final mass}$$

highlights.text: Initial mass of sample and container

steps.title: Calculate actual water mass
steps.content:
Using the data from the table, let's calculate the mass of water actually lost in the experiment:

$$\text{Mass of water lost} = 25.825\text{ g} - 23.977\text{ g} = 1.848\text{ g}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between the initial and final mass from the data table.

highlights.text: Initial mass of sample and container

steps.title: Substitute hypothetical spilled mass
steps.content:
If some solid is spilled during heating, the final mass measured by the student will be **lower** than it should be, because both water *and* some solid are missing. 

Let's apply a hypothetical lower final mass of $23.000\text{ g}$ (instead of the correct $23.977\text{ g}$) to see the effect.

steps.title: Calculate resulting water mass
steps.content:
Now we re-calculate the mass of water lost using this lower final mass value:

$$\text{Calculated water lost} = 25.825\text{ g} - 23.000\text{ g} = 2.825\text{ g}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the mass lost with the hypothetical lower final mass.

steps.title: Analyze the result
steps.content:
Comparing the two results:
- Actual mass lost: $1.848\text{ g}$
- Calculated mass lost with spill: $2.825\text{ g}$

Because $2.825 > 1.848$, we can see that the calculated mass of water lost is **too large**. The spill is incorrectly counted as additional water leaving the crucible.

parts.content:
In a second experiment, the student is given a mixture containing anhydrous $MgCl_2$ and $KNO_3$ with a mass of $2.94 \text{ g}$. To determine the percentage by mass of $MgCl_2$ in the mixture, the student uses excess $AgNO_3(aq)$ to precipitate the chloride ions as $AgCl(s)$. 

Suggest how the insoluble $AgCl(s)$ could be separated from an aqueous solution.
parts.markscheme: The insoluble solid could be separated by filtration. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the states of matter
steps.content: To choose the correct separation technique, we first look at the physical states of the components in the mixture. The question mentions that we have an "insoluble" substance, $AgCl(s)$, which is a solid precipitate, and an "aqueous solution" containing the remaining ions.

steps.title: Select the separation method
steps.content:
In chemistry, the standard method for separating an insoluble solid from a liquid (or solution) is **filtration**. This technique relies on the difference in particle size: the large solid particles cannot pass through the pores of the filter medium, while the liquid and dissolved ions can.

This is a fundamental technique covered in **Topic S1.1: Introduction to the particulate nature of matter** regarding the separation of mixtures.

steps.title: Describe the filtration process
steps.content:
To perform the separation:
1. Place a piece of **filter paper** inside a **funnel**.
2. Position the funnel over a collection vessel (like a beaker or flask).
3. Pour the mixture into the funnel.

The $AgCl(s)$ will remain on the filter paper as the **residue**, while the liquid passes through as the **filtrate**.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The insoluble silver chloride can be separated from the aqueous solution by **filtration**.

As noted in the markscheme, simply stating the method is enough to earn the mark.

highlights.text: The insoluble solid could be separated by filtration.

steps.title: Analyze the mixture
steps.content: In this experiment, the student has produced a precipitate of silver chloride, $AgCl(s)$. This is an **insoluble solid** suspended in an aqueous solution. To determine the mass of $MgCl_2$, we first need to physically separate this solid from the liquid phase.

highlights.text: insoluble $AgCl(s)$ could be separated from an aqueous solution

steps.title: Apply centrifugation
steps.content:
One effective way to separate a solid from a liquid is **centrifugation**. This involves placing the mixture into a centrifuge tube and spinning it at very high speeds. 

This technique is a common alternative to filtration, especially when dealing with very fine precipitates that might pass through filter paper. You can find more about separating mixtures in **Topic S1.1.1: Elements, Compounds and Mixtures**.

steps.title: Understand the process
steps.content: During rotation, the high-speed motion creates a centrifugal force that pushes the denser $AgCl(s)$ particles to the bottom of the tube. After a few minutes, the solid forms a compact **pellet** at the bottom, while the liquid above it, known as the **supernatant**, becomes clear.

steps.title: Final separation
steps.content: Once the solid has settled at the bottom, the student can carefully pour off (decant) the supernatant liquid or remove it using a pipette. This leaves the pure $AgCl(s)$ behind in the tube, ready to be dried and weighed.

parts.content: The mass of $AgCl(s)$ is found to be $5.48 \text{ g}$. Determine the amount (in mol) of $AgCl(s)$ and the amount (in mol) of $MgCl_2$ in the original mixture.
parts.markscheme:
Amount (in mol) of $AgCl = 5.48 / 143.32 = 0.0382 \text{ mol}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Amount (in mol) of $MgCl_2 = 0.0382 / 2  = 0.0191 \text{ mol}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we need to convert the experimental mass of the silver chloride precipitate into a chemical amount (moles). We are given:

*   Mass of $AgCl(s) = 5.48 \text{ g}$
*   Molar mass ($M$) of $AgCl = 143.32 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$ (calculated from the periodic table: $107.87 + 35.45$)

highlights.text: The mass of AgCl(s) is found to be 5.48 g

steps.title: Calculate moles of AgCl
steps.content:
Using the formula from the data booklet, we calculate the amount ($n$) of silver chloride by dividing the mass ($m$) by the molar mass ($M$):

$$\begin{aligned} n(AgCl) &= \frac{m}{M} \\ n(AgCl) &= \frac{5.48 \text{ g}}{143.32 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n(AgCl) &\approx 0.0382 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns the first mark.

calculator.description: Divide mass by molar mass to find moles.

steps.title: Apply stoichiometric ratio
steps.content:
Next, we relate the amount of $AgCl$ to the amount of $MgCl_2$. 

Each formula unit of magnesium chloride ($MgCl_2$) contains **two** chloride ions. When it reacts with silver nitrate, it forms two units of silver chloride:

$$MgCl_2(aq) + 2AgNO_3(aq) \rightarrow 2AgCl(s) + Mg(NO_3)_2(aq)$$

The mole ratio is $1 \text{ mol } MgCl_2 : 2 \text{ mol } AgCl$. This concept is covered in **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

steps.title: Calculate moles of MgCl2
steps.content:
Since the ratio is $1:2$, we divide the moles of $AgCl$ by $2$ to find the moles of $MgCl_2$ in the original mixture:

$$\begin{aligned} n(MgCl_2) &= \frac{n(AgCl)}{2} \\ n(MgCl_2) &= \frac{0.0382 \text{ mol}}{2} \\ n(MgCl_2) &= 0.0191 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

This final step completes the calculation and earns the second mark.

gdc.instructions: Divide the molar amount of AgCl by 2.

calculator.description: Divide the previous result by 2 to account for the 1:2 ratio.

parts.content: Determine the percentage composition by mass of $MgCl_2$ in the original mixture.
parts.markscheme:
Amount (in mol) of $AgCl = 5.48 / 143.32 = 0.0382 \text{ mol}$

Amount (in mol) of $MgCl_2 = 0.0382 / 2 = 0.0191 \text{ mol}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Mass of $MgCl_2 = 0.0191 \times 95.20 = 1.82 \text{ g}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Percentage $= (1.82 / 2.94) \times 100 = 61.9 \%$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

questions.specification:
4.22 g of magnesium carbonate is decomposed by heating until constant mass. Use atomic masses from the IB Chemistry data booklet.

$$\mathrm{MgCO_3 (s) \rightarrow MgO (s) + CO_2 (g)}$$

What is the final mass of solid?

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the balanced decomposition equation MgCO3 → MgO + CO2 to apply mole ratios and determine amount (and hence mass) of MgO produced from a given mass of MgCO3.

questions.specification:
The combustion of propane proceeds according to the chemical equation provided below:

$$\mathrm{C_3H_8}(g) + 5\mathrm{O_2}(g) \rightarrow 3\mathrm{CO_2}(g) + 4\mathrm{H_2O}(l)$$

The following table shows the initial volumes of the two reactants used in a reaction vessel:

| Reactant | Initial volume / $\text{cm}^3$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Propane ($\mathrm{C_3H_8}$) | $20.0$ |
| Oxygen ($\mathrm{O_2}$) | $120.0$ |

What is the total volume of gas remaining in the vessel after the reaction is complete and the mixture has cooled to room temperature, including any unreacted reactants?

Assume all gas volumes are measured at the same temperature and pressure.

questions.specification: What is the concentration of bromide ions, in $\mathrm{mol\,dm}^{-3}$, in a solution formed by mixing $200\ \mathrm{cm}^3$ of $2\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$ $\text{LiBr}$ with $300\ \mathrm{cm}^3$ of $12\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$ $\text{NaBr}$? (Convert $\mathrm{cm}^3$ to $\mathrm{dm}^3$.)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.4 Counting particles by mass: The mole > S1.4.5 Molar concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.5 Molar concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the final bromide ion concentration after mixing two solutions, requiring calculation of moles from given molarities and volumes and dividing by total volume (dilution/mixing of solutions).

options.markscheme: Incorrect: after converting volumes to $\mathrm{dm}^3$ and summing moles of $\mathrm{Br^-}$, the concentration is not $2\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$.
Incorrect: total moles of $\mathrm{Br^-}$ divided by total volume gives $8.0\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$, not $7\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$.
Incorrect: $14\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$ would exceed the highest starting concentration and does not match $n/V$ after mixing.

Correct: $200\ \mathrm{cm}^3=0.200\ \mathrm{dm}^3$, $300\ \mathrm{cm}^3=0.300\ \mathrm{dm}^3$, total volume $=0.500\ \mathrm{dm}^3$. Moles $\mathrm{Br^-}$ from $\text{LiBr}=(2)(0.200)=0.400$ mol; from $\text{NaBr}=(12)(0.300)=3.600$ mol; total $=4.000$ mol. $[\mathrm{Br^-}]=4.000/0.500=8.0\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$.

questions.specification:
An industrial chemist monitors the production of chlorine gas in an electrolytic cell containing an aqueous solution of a metal chloride, $X\text{Cl}_2$. During a test run, a constant current of $0.750\text{ A}$ was passed through the cell. At the anode, $360\text{ cm}^3$ of chlorine gas was collected, measured at room temperature and pressure (RTP). Simultaneously, $0.953\text{ g}$ of metal $X$ was deposited on the cathode.

(Molar volume of a gas at RTP $= 24.0\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1}$; Faraday constant $F = 96500\text{ C mol}^{-1}$)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.2.8 Electrolytic cells
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario is electrolysis in an electrolytic cell producing Cl2 at the anode and depositing a metal at the cathode; parts involve electrons transferred and time from current (Faraday’s laws context).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.2.2 Redox half-equations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires writing the anode half-equation for chloride oxidation to chlorine gas and relating electrons to products formed.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.5.3 Molar volume of an ideal gas
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) converts collected chlorine gas volume at RTP to moles using the molar volume at RTP (24.0 dm3 mol−1).

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) calculates relative atomic mass of metal X from mass deposited and moles of X derived from electron count/stoichiometry, then identifies the metal.

parts.content: Calculate the amount, in mol, of chlorine gas produced at the anode during the electrolysis.
parts.markscheme:
- Convert the gas volume to $\text{dm}^3$: $V = \dfrac{360}{1000} = 0.360\text{ dm}^3$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State or use the molar volume relationship: $n(\text{Cl}_2) = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the final value: $n(\text{Cl}_2) = \dfrac{0.360\text{ dm}^3}{24.0\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1}} = 0.0150\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks for the correct final answer.</note>

parts.content: State the half-equation for the formation of chlorine gas at the anode.
parts.markscheme:
- $2\text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2(g) + 2e^-$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept equations showing $e^-$ on the left if the charge is balanced correctly, but this is the standard oxidation representation.</note>

parts.content: Determine the total amount, in mol, of electrons that passed through the circuit during this process.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the $1:2$ ratio between $\text{Cl}_2$ and $e^-$ from the half-equation: $n(e^-) = 2 \times n(\text{Cl}_2)$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the amount of electrons: $2 \times 0.0150\text{ mol} = 0.0300\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the duration of the electrolysis process in minutes.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the total charge passed: $Q = n(e^-) \times F = 0.0300\text{ mol} \times 96500\text{ C mol}^{-1} = 2895\text{ C}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the time in seconds: $t = \dfrac{Q}{I} = \dfrac{2895\text{ C}}{0.750\text{ A}} = 3860\text{ s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Convert the time to minutes: $\dfrac{3860}{60} = 64.3\text{ min}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the relative atomic mass of metal $X$ and identify the metal using the data provided.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine the moles of $X$ deposited (using $X^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow X$): $n(X) = \dfrac{0.0300}{2} = 0.0150\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the molar mass and identify the metal: $M = \dfrac{0.953\text{ g}}{0.0150\text{ mol}} = 63.53\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. Therefore, $X$ is Copper/$\text{Cu}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What is the resulting concentration, in $\mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$, when $2.0\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.200\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}$ potassium hydroxide solution is diluted to $50.0\text{ cm}^3$ with water? (You may use $c_2=c_1\times\frac{V_1}{V_2}$ with volumes in the same units.)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.4 Counting particles by mass: The mole > S1.4.5 Molar concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.5 Molar concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is a dilution calculation requiring use of molar concentration (mol dm^-3), converting volumes (cm^3 to dm^3) and applying conservation of moles (C1V1 = C2V2).

options.markscheme: Uses incorrect dilution factor (e.g. $\frac{V_2}{V_1}$ or other arithmetic error).
Typically corresponds to an extra factor of 2 error (e.g. taking $V_1=1.0\text{ cm}^3$ or doubling $V_2$).

Correct: $c_2=c_1\times\frac{V_1}{V_2}=0.200\times\frac{2.0}{50.0}=0.0080\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$ (equivalently, conserve moles: $n=0.200\times0.0020=4.00\times10^{-4}$ mol; $c_2=\frac{n}{0.0500}=0.00800$).
Typically corresponds to using $V_2=5.0\text{ cm}^3$ or misplacing a decimal point in the dilution ratio.

questions.specification: An organic compound is analyzed to determine its empirical formula. It is composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. Complete combustion of $1.50\text{ g}$ of the compound produces $3.30\text{ g}$ of carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) and $1.35\text{ g}$ of water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.4 Empirical and molecular formulas

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.1.1 Chemical equations and stoichiometry
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (c)–(e) require stoichiometric calculations converting masses of CO2 and H2O to moles of C and H, then using mass differences to find O and derive ratios.

parts.content: Define the term empirical formula.
parts.markscheme: The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Prerequisite Knowledge
steps.content: To answer this question, we look at **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**, which covers the relationship between empirical and molecular formulas. While a molecular formula shows the actual number of atoms in a molecule, the empirical formula represents the fundamental ratio between them.

steps.title: Define Empirical Formula
steps.content:
The formal definition of an **empirical formula** is:

The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element present in a compound.

This means that if you have a compound like glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$), its empirical formula is the reduced version, $CH_2O$.

steps.title: Awarding of Marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, to earn the mark for this definition, you must include two specific criteria:
1. The ratio must be the **simplest**.
2. The ratio must consist of **whole numbers**.

You can refer to the ratio of "atoms" or "moles" of each element.

highlights.text: The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound.

parts.content: Calculate the moles of carbon and hydrogen present in the compound based on the combustion data.
parts.markscheme:
**Step 1: Moles of carbon in $\text{CO}_2$**

$$\text{Moles of } \text{CO}_2 = \frac{3.30\text{ g}}{44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0750\text{ mol}$$

Since each $\text{CO}_2$ molecule contains one carbon atom:
$$\text{Moles of C} = 0.0750\text{ mol}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Step 2: Moles of hydrogen in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$**

$$\text{Moles of } \text{H}_2\text{O} = \frac{1.35\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0749\text{ mol}$$

Since each $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ molecule contains two hydrogen atoms:
$$\text{Moles of H} = 0.0749\text{ mol} \times 2 = 0.150\text{ mol}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
To find the moles of carbon and hydrogen in the compound, we use the fact that all carbon in the compound ends up in $\text{CO}_2$ and all hydrogen ends up in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ during complete combustion. We will use the molar mass of these products to work backward to the elements. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**, specifically regarding empirical formulas.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Carbon
steps.content:
First, we find the moles of $\text{CO}_2$ produced using the formula from the data booklet:

$$\text{moles } (n) = \frac{\text{mass } (m)}{\text{molar mass } (M)}$$

The molar mass of $\text{CO}_2$ is $44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. Since there is $1$ atom of carbon in every molecule of $\text{CO}_2$, the moles of carbon will be equal to the moles of $\text{CO}_2$.

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{CO}_2) &= \frac{3.30\text{ g}}{44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n(\text{C}) &= 0.0750\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the mass divided by the molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate moles of carbon dioxide produced.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, we find the moles of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ produced. The molar mass of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is $18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. 

Crucially, each molecule of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ contains **two** hydrogen atoms. Therefore, the moles of hydrogen ($n(\text{H})$) is double the moles of water:

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) &= \frac{1.35\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0749\text{ mol} \\ n(\text{H}) &= 0.0749\text{ mol} \times 2 \\ n(\text{H}) &= 0.150\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the mass by molar mass, then multiply the result by 2.

desmos.instructions: Input (1.35 / 18.02) * 2 to get the total moles of hydrogen.

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of hydrogen atoms by multiplying the moles of water by 2.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
Based on the Direct Molar Conversion method:
- **Moles of C:** $0.0750\text{ mol}$
- **Moles of H:** $0.150\text{ mol}$

In an IB exam, you earn 1 mark for each correctly calculated molar value, provided you show the 1:1 ratio for Carbon and the 2:1 ratio for Hydrogen.

steps.title: Calculate mass of Carbon
steps.content:
To find the mass of carbon in the compound, we use the mass fraction of carbon in $\text{CO}_2$. Since carbon has an atomic mass of $12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ and $\text{CO}_2$ has a molar mass of $44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$, the fraction of mass that is carbon is $\frac{12.01}{44.01}$.

$$\text{mass of C} = 3.30\text{ g} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} \approx 0.901\text{ g}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of carbon from the mass of carbon dioxide.

steps.title: Convert Carbon to moles
steps.content:
Now, we convert the mass of carbon into moles using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\text{moles of C} = \frac{0.901\text{ g}}{12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0750\text{ mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of carbon.

steps.title: Calculate mass of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, we find the mass of hydrogen using its mass fraction in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Since there are two hydrogen atoms ($2 \times 1.01 = 2.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$) in water ($18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$), the fraction is $\frac{2.02}{18.02}$.

$$\text{mass of H} = 1.35\text{ g} \times \frac{2.02}{18.02} \approx 0.151\text{ g}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of hydrogen from the mass of water.

steps.title: Convert Hydrogen to moles
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the mass of hydrogen into moles. Note that we divide by the atomic mass of H ($1.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$) because the empirical formula is based on the ratio of individual atoms.

$$\text{moles of H} = \frac{0.151\text{ g}}{1.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.150\text{ mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of hydrogen.

parts.content: Determine the mass of oxygen in the organic compound.
parts.markscheme:
**Mass of carbon:**
$$\text{Mass of C} = 0.0750\text{ mol} \times 12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.901\text{ g}$$

**Mass of hydrogen:**
$$\text{Mass of H} = 0.150\text{ mol} \times 1.008\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.151\text{ g}$$

**Mass of oxygen:**
$$\text{Mass of O} = 1.50\text{ g} - (0.901\text{ g} + 0.151\text{ g}) = 0.448\text{ g}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate mass of Carbon
steps.content:
First, we need to find the mass of carbon in the original sample. All the carbon from the compound ends up in the $3.30\text{ g}$ of $\text{CO}_2$. We use the formula for moles from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

1. Find moles of $\text{CO}_2$: 
$$n(\text{CO}_2) = \frac{3.30\text{ g}}{44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0750\text{ mol}$$

2. Since each $\text{CO}_2$ molecule has one carbon atom, $n(\text{C}) = 0.0750\text{ mol}$. Now convert back to mass:
$$\text{Mass of C} = 0.0750\text{ mol} \times 12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.901\text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide 3.30 by 44.01 to get moles, then multiply by 12.01.

calculator.description: Calculate mass of carbon from CO2

steps.title: Calculate mass of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, we find the mass of hydrogen. All the hydrogen from the compound ends up in the $1.35\text{ g}$ of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

1. Find moles of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$:
$$n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = \frac{1.35\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0750\text{ mol}$$

2. Since each water molecule has **two** hydrogen atoms, the moles of H atoms is double:
$$n(\text{H}) = 2 \times 0.0750\text{ mol} = 0.150\text{ mol}$$

3. Convert to mass:
$$\text{Mass of H} = 0.150\text{ mol} \times 1.008\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.151\text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide 1.35 by 18.02, multiply by 2, then multiply by 1.008.

calculator.description: Calculate mass of hydrogen from H2O

steps.title: Subtract to find Oxygen
steps.content:
Since the organic compound is only composed of C, H, and O, we can find the mass of oxygen by subtracting the masses of C and H from the total mass of the compound ($1.50\text{ g}$).

$$\text{Mass of O} = \text{Total mass} - (\text{Mass of C} + \text{Mass of H})$$
$$\text{Mass of O} = 1.50\text{ g} - (0.901\text{ g} + 0.151\text{ g})$$
$$\text{Mass of O} = 0.448\text{ g}$$

This logic follows **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**, where we use mass conservation to identify unknown components.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the sum of 0.901 and 0.151 from 1.50.

calculator.description: Subtract C and H masses from total mass

highlights.text: \text{Mass of O} = 1.50\text{ g} - (0.901\text{ g} + 0.151\text{ g}) = 0.448\text{ g}

steps.title: Calculate mass of carbon
steps.content:
To find the mass of carbon in the original compound, we use the mass fraction of carbon in $\text{CO}_2$. 

The molar mass of $\text{CO}_2$ is $12.01 + 2(16.00) = 44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. 

$$\text{Mass of C} = \text{Mass of } \text{CO}_2 \times \frac{\text{Molar Mass of C}}{\text{Molar Mass of } \text{CO}_2}$$

$$\text{Mass of C} = 3.30\text{ g} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} = 0.901\text{ g}$$

calculator.description: Calculate mass of carbon from CO2

steps.title: Calculate mass of hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the mass of hydrogen using the mass fraction of hydrogen in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. 

The molar mass of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is $2(1.008) + 16.00 = 18.016\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. Note that there are 2 moles of hydrogen per mole of water.

$$\text{Mass of H} = \text{Mass of } \text{H}_2\text{O} \times \frac{2 \times \text{Molar Mass of H}}{\text{Molar Mass of } \text{H}_2\text{O}}$$

$$\text{Mass of H} = 1.35\text{ g} \times \frac{2.016}{18.016} = 0.151\text{ g}$$

calculator.description: Calculate mass of hydrogen from H2O

steps.title: Calculate mass of oxygen
steps.content:
Since the compound only contains carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, the mass of oxygen is the remaining mass of the $1.50\text{ g}$ sample after subtracting the carbon and hydrogen.

$$\text{Mass of O} = \text{Total Mass} - (\text{Mass of C} + \text{Mass of H})$$

$$\text{Mass of O} = 1.50\text{ g} - (0.901\text{ g} + 0.151\text{ g}) = 0.448\text{ g}$$

calculator.description: Calculate mass of oxygen by subtraction

parts.content: Calculate the empirical formula of the organic compound.
parts.markscheme:
**Step 1: Moles of each element**
*   C: $0.0750\text{ mol}$
*   H: $0.150\text{ mol}$
*   O: $\frac{0.448\text{ g}}{16.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0280\text{ mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Step 2: Divide by the smallest number of moles ($0.0280$)**
*   C: $\frac{0.0750}{0.0280} = 2.68$
*   H: $\frac{0.150}{0.0280} = 5.36$
*   O: $\frac{0.0280}{0.0280} = 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Step 3: Convert to whole numbers**
Multiply by 3:
*   C: $2.68 \times 3 \approx 8$
*   H: $5.36 \times 3 \approx 16$
*   O: $1 \times 3 = 3$

Empirical formula: $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{16}\text{O}_3$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate mass of Carbon
steps.content:
Using the mass and mole analysis approach, we first find the mass of carbon from the $3.30\text{ g}$ of $\text{CO}_2$ produced. Since all the carbon in the original sample ends up in the $\text{CO}_2$, we use the mass ratio of C to $\text{CO}_2$:

$$\text{Mass of C} = \text{mass of CO}_2 \times \frac{M_r(\text{C})}{M_r(\text{CO}_2)}$$

$$\text{Mass of C} = 3.30\text{ g} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} = 0.9006\text{ g}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the mass of CO2 by the fraction of carbon's molar mass over total CO2 molar mass.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication of the mass by the molar mass ratio.

desmos.instructions: Type the calculation directly to find the mass of carbon.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of carbon in the CO2 sample.

steps.title: Calculate mass of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, find the mass of hydrogen from the $1.35\text{ g}$ of water produced. Note that there are two hydrogen atoms in every water molecule ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$):

$$\text{Mass of H} = \text{mass of H}_2\text{O} \times \frac{2 \times M_r(\text{H})}{M_r(\text{H}_2\text{O})}$$

$$\text{Mass of H} = 1.35\text{ g} \times \frac{2.02}{18.02} = 0.1513\text{ g}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the fraction of hydrogen in H2O and multiply by the total water mass.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the mass of water by the hydrogen mass ratio.

desmos.instructions: Multiply 1.35 by the ratio of (2*1.01) to 18.02.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of hydrogen in the water sample.

steps.title: Calculate mass of Oxygen
steps.content:
The compound is made of C, H, and O. To find the mass of oxygen, subtract the masses of C and H from the initial sample mass of $1.50\text{ g}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Mass of O} &= \text{Total mass} - (\text{Mass C} + \text{Mass H}) \\ \text{Mass of O} &= 1.50\text{ g} - (0.9006\text{ g} + 0.1513\text{ g}) \\ \text{Mass of O} &= 0.4481\text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the found element masses from the sample mass.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate 1.50 minus the sum of the previously calculated C and H masses.

calculator.description: Subtract the masses of C and H from the total mass.

steps.title: Convert masses to moles
steps.content:
Now, convert these masses into moles using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\begin{aligned} n_C &= \frac{0.9006}{12.01} = 0.0750\text{ mol} \\ n_H &= \frac{0.1513}{1.01} = 0.150\text{ mol} \\ n_O &= \frac{0.4481}{16.00} = 0.0280\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine simple molar ratio
steps.content:
Divide each amount by the smallest number of moles ($0.0280$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C} &: \frac{0.0750}{0.0280} = 2.68 \\ \text{H} &: \frac{0.150}{0.0280} = 5.36 \\ \text{O} &: \frac{0.0280}{0.0280} = 1.00 \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Find the ratio of C and H relative to O.

calculator.description: Divide moles of each element by the smallest value.

steps.title: Convert to whole numbers
steps.content:
Since $2.68$ and $5.36$ are not whole numbers, we need to find a multiplier. Notice that $2.68 \approx 2\frac{2}{3} = \frac{8}{3}$. Multiplying by $3$ will give us whole numbers:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C} &: 2.68 \times 3 \approx 8 \\ \text{H} &: 5.36 \times 3 \approx 16 \\ \text{O} &: 1 \times 3 = 3 \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the empirical formula is $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{16}\text{O}_3$.

gdc.instructions: Scale the ratio to whole numbers.

desmos.instructions: Verify the result of multiplying the ratios by 3.

calculator.description: Multiply ratios by 3 to reach integers.

steps.title: Calculate percentage of Carbon
steps.content:
First, we find the mass of Carbon in the produced $\text{CO}_2$. Since all the Carbon in the original compound ends up in $\text{CO}_2$, we use the molar mass ratio ($M_{\text{C}} = 12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ and $M_{\text{CO}_2} = 44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$):

$$\text{Mass of C} = 3.30\text{ g} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} = 0.9005\text{ g}$$

Now, convert this to a percentage of the original $1.50\text{ g}$ compound:

$$\%\text{C} = \left( \frac{0.9005\text{ g}}{1.50\text{ g}} \right) \times 100 = 60.03\%$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the mass of C using 3.30 * (12.01 / 44.01). Calculate the percentage using (Ans / 1.50) * 100.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.30 * (12.01 / 44.01) to find the mass of C. Then divide that result by 1.50 and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Input the expression to find the mass percentage directly.

calculator.description: Calculate mass and percentage of Carbon

steps.title: Calculate percentage of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, find the mass of Hydrogen in the produced $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. There are 2 Hydrogen atoms per water molecule ($M_{\text{H}} = 1.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ and $M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$):

$$\text{Mass of H} = 1.35\text{ g} \times \frac{2 \times 1.01}{18.02} = 0.1513\text{ g}$$

Convert this to a percentage of the original $1.50\text{ g}$ compound:

$$\%\text{H} = \left( \frac{0.1513\text{ g}}{1.50\text{ g}} \right) \times 100 = 10.09\%$$

gdc.instructions: Find the mass of H: 1.35 * (2.02 / 18.02). Then calculate percentage: (Ans / 1.50) * 100.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.35 * (2.02 / 18.02) to find the mass of H. Divide by 1.50 and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression for percentage of hydrogen.

calculator.description: Calculate mass and percentage of Hydrogen

steps.title: Find percentage of Oxygen
steps.content:
Since the compound only contains C, H, and O, the remaining percentage must be Oxygen:

$$\%\text{O} = 100\% - \%\text{C} - \%\text{H}$$
$$\%\text{O} = 100 - 60.03 - 10.09 = 29.88\%$$

calculator.description: Find the remaining percentage for Oxygen

steps.title: Determine molar ratios
steps.content:
To find the empirical formula, we treat these percentages as masses in a $100\text{ g}$ sample and convert to moles using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{C}) &= \frac{60.03}{12.01} = 4.998 \text{ mol} \\ n(\text{H}) &= \frac{10.09}{1.01} = 9.990 \text{ mol} \\ n(\text{O}) &= \frac{29.88}{16.00} = 1.868 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

Now, divide each by the smallest number of moles ($1.868$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C:} & \frac{4.998}{1.868} \approx 2.68 \\ \text{H:} & \frac{9.990}{1.868} \approx 5.35 \\ \text{O:} & \frac{1.868}{1.868} = 1 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Find the mole values, then divide by the smallest one to find the ratio.

ti84.instructions: Divide each percentage by its molar mass to get moles. Then divide those results by the smallest value (approx 1.868).

desmos.instructions: Divide the calculated moles to find the ratio for C and H.

calculator.description: Calculate molar ratios

steps.title: Scale to whole numbers
steps.content:
The ratios $2.68 : 5.35 : 1$ are not whole numbers. Since $0.68$ is approximately $2/3$, we multiply the entire ratio by $3$ to obtain whole numbers:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C:} & 2.68 \times 3 \approx 8 \\ \text{H:} & 5.35 \times 3 \approx 16 \\ \text{O:} & 1 \times 3 = 3 \end{aligned}$$

The empirical formula is $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{16}\text{O}_3$.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the ratios by 3 to verify they reach whole numbers.

calculator.description: Final scaling to whole numbers

parts.content: Explain how molecular formula differs from empirical formula, and describe one method to determine the molecular formula from experimental data.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition**
The molecular formula represents the actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule, while the empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of the elements. <marks>A1</marks>

**Determination method**
*   Measure the compound's molar mass experimentally (e.g., using mass spectrometry). <marks>A1</marks>
*   Divide the experimental molar mass by the molar mass of the empirical formula to find the integer multiplier and multiply the empirical formula by this factor. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define Formula Types
steps.content:
To start, we need to distinguish between these two fundamental chemistry terms. 

*   **Empirical Formula**: This is the simplest whole-number ratio of the atoms of each element in a compound. 
*   **Molecular Formula**: This represents the actual number of atoms of each element present in one molecule of the compound.

This concept is covered in **Topic S1.4.4: Empirical and molecular formulas**. Think of the empirical formula as the "reduced" version of the molecular formula.

highlights.text: The molecular formula represents the actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule, while the empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of the elements.

steps.title: Determine Experimental Molar Mass
steps.content:
To find the molecular formula from an empirical one, we must determine the compound's actual molar mass experimentally. 

A standard method for this is **mass spectrometry** (covered in **Topic S3.2.8**). When a sample is analyzed, we look for the **molecular ion peak** ($M^+$), which corresponds to the entire molecule losing one electron. The mass-to-charge ratio ($m/z$) of this peak gives us the relative molecular mass ($M_r$) of the compound.

highlights.text: Measure the compound's molar mass experimentally (e.g., using mass spectrometry).

steps.title: Calculate the Multiplier
steps.content:
Once we have the experimental molar mass and the mass of the empirical formula, we find the integer multiplier ($n$).

We use the formula:

$$n = \frac{\text{Molar Mass (from mass spectrometry)}}{\text{Molar Mass (of empirical formula)}}$$

This $n$ value tells us how many empirical units fit into the actual molecule.

highlights.text: Divide the experimental molar mass by the molar mass of the empirical formula to find the integer multiplier

steps.title: Apply the Multiplier
steps.content:
The final step is to multiply all the subscripts in the empirical formula by the integer $n$ we just calculated. 

$$\text{Molecular Formula} = n \times (\text{Empirical Formula})$$

According to the IB markscheme, clearly stating this relationship and how to find $n$ is essential for earning full marks.

highlights.text: multiply the empirical formula by this factor.

steps.title: Contrast the formulas
steps.content:
The **empirical formula** is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound. In contrast, the **molecular formula** represents the actual number of atoms of each element present in one molecule of the compound.

This is a fundamental concept in **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**.

highlights.text: The molecular formula represents the actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule, while the empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of the elements.

steps.title: Experimental molar mass determination
steps.content:
To find the molecular formula, we first need the experimental molar mass ($M$). Using the **Ideal Gas Law**, we can vaporize a known mass ($m$) of the compound and measure its pressure ($P$), volume ($V$), and temperature ($T$). 

From the data booklet, we use the ideal gas equation:

$$PV = nRT$$

Since the number of moles is defined as $n = \frac{m}{M}$, we can substitute and rearrange to solve for the molar mass:

$$\begin{aligned} PV &= \frac{m}{M}RT \\ M &= \frac{mRT}{PV} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the molecular formula
steps.content:
Once the experimental molar mass is determined, compare it to the molar mass of the empirical formula ($M_{\text{empirical}}$). 

Find the integer multiplier ($k$) using this ratio:

$$k = \frac{M_{\text{experimental}}}{M_{\text{empirical}}}$$

Finally, multiply all subscripts in the empirical formula by $k$ to obtain the molecular formula.

parts.content: Outline two products which would form as a result of incomplete combustion.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>A1</marks> for each of the following (max 2 marks):
*   Carbon monoxide / $\text{CO}$
*   Soot / carbon particles / $\text{C}$

steps.title: Define incomplete combustion
steps.content:
In chemistry, **combustion** is the reaction of a fuel with oxygen. While complete combustion occurs when oxygen is in excess (producing $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$), **incomplete combustion** occurs when the oxygen supply is limited.

This topic is covered in **Reactivity 1.3: Energy from fuels**, specifically section **R1.3.2 Incomplete combustion**.

steps.title: Identify carbon monoxide
steps.content:
When there is not enough oxygen to fully oxidize the carbon atoms to carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$), one characteristic product is **carbon monoxide** ($$CO$$).

Carbon monoxide is a colorless, odorless, and toxic gas. In an IB exam, you can provide either the name or the chemical formula to earn the mark.

steps.title: Identify soot (carbon)
steps.content:
If the oxygen supply is extremely limited, the carbon atoms may not be oxidized at all, resulting in the formation of solid **carbon** ($$C$$), commonly referred to as **soot**.

Soot appears as black smoke or a powdery deposit during the reaction. Just like with carbon monoxide, either the name "soot", "carbon particles", or the symbol "C" is acceptable.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks available for this question, you should list the two carbon-containing products that differ from those of complete combustion:

1.  **Carbon monoxide** (or $$\text{CO}$$)
2.  **Soot** (or **carbon** / $$\text{C}$$)

Note: While water ($$\text{H}_2\text{O}$$) is also a product of incomplete combustion, examiners typically look for the carbon-based products ($$\text{CO}$$ or $$\text{C}$$) because they are specific to the "incomplete" process.

steps.title: Define incomplete combustion
steps.content:
Incomplete combustion occurs when there is an insufficient supply of oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) to fully oxidize the carbon in the fuel to carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$). This concept is covered in **Reactivity 1.3: Energy from fuels (R1.3.2)**. 

When the oxygen-to-fuel ratio drops, the carbon atoms form partially oxidized species or remain unoxidized.

steps.title: Derive product 1: CO
steps.content:
If we consider a simple carbohydrate-like compound with the empirical formula $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$ (consistent with the masses provided in the problem), we can compare the oxygen requirements.

For **complete combustion**, the ratio is 1:1:
$$\text{CH}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

If the oxygen supply is limited (e.g., a 1:0.5 ratio), the carbon is only partially oxidized to **carbon monoxide**:
$$\text{CH}_2\text{O} + 0.5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Derive product 2: C
steps.content:
When the oxygen supply is extremely limited, the oxygen available may only be enough to react with the hydrogen to form water, leaving the carbon unreacted. 

In this scenario, solid **carbon** (often seen as soot) is produced:
$$\text{CH}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Note that even without adding external $\text{O}_2$, the oxygen already present within the organic compound's structure can facilitate the formation of water, leaving elemental carbon behind.

steps.title: Identify final products
steps.content:
Based on the chemical equations derived for limited oxygen conditions, the two products formed are:

1.  **Carbon monoxide** ($\text{CO}$)
2.  **Carbon** ($\text{C}$) / soot / carbon particles

Each correct identification earns 1 mark according to the markscheme.

parts.content: State one major problem during combustion experiments.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>A1</marks> for any of the following:
*   Formation of soot/carbon particles (due to incomplete combustion)
*   Formation of carbon monoxide (toxic gas)
*   Difficulty in ensuring complete combustion
*   Difficulty in collecting all gaseous products / gas leaks

steps.title: Ideal combustion assumption
steps.content: In an ideal combustion analysis experiment, we assume that every single atom of carbon in the original $1.50\text{ g}$ sample reacts fully with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$). Similarly, we assume all hydrogen atoms form water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$). This complete reaction is essential for accurately calculating the empirical formula, as covered in **Topic S1.4.4: Empirical and molecular formulas**.

steps.title: Identifying incomplete combustion
steps.content: In practice, it is often very difficult to ensure that the reaction goes to 100% completion. This is known as **incomplete combustion**, which usually occurs if the oxygen supply is insufficient or the temperature is not high enough. This concept is explored further in **Topic R1.3.2: Incomplete combustion**.

steps.title: Formation of by-products
steps.content:
When incomplete combustion occurs, carbon atoms do not all turn into $\text{CO}_2$. Instead, some may form:

*   **Carbon monoxide ($\text{CO}$)**: A colorless, odorless, and toxic gas.
*   **Soot ($\text{C}$)**: Fine black particles of solid carbon.

Because these products are not measured as $\text{CO}_2$, the amount of carbon in the compound will be underestimated, leading to an incorrect empirical formula.

steps.title: State the problem
steps.content: A major problem in these experiments is the formation of soot or carbon monoxide due to the difficulty in ensuring complete combustion.

highlights.text: Formation of soot/carbon particles (due to incomplete combustion)

steps.title: Analyze the experimental goal
steps.content: In a combustion analysis experiment, we calculate the empirical formula based on the masses of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ produced. This method relies on the assumption that **all** the carbon in the sample is converted into carbon dioxide and **all** the hydrogen is converted into water. Any physical or technical issue that prevents this will lead to an incorrect result.

highlights.text: State one major problem during combustion experiments.

steps.title: Incomplete combustion limitations
steps.content:
A primary technical difficulty is **incomplete combustion**. This occurs if there is insufficient oxygen or if the temperature isn't high enough. 

Instead of forming only $\text{CO}_2$, some carbon may form:
*   **Carbon monoxide (CO)**: A colorless gas that escapes detection if only $\text{CO}_2$ is being measured.
*   **Soot (C)**: Solid carbon particles that remain in the combustion tube.

If this happens, the measured mass of $\text{CO}_2$ will be lower than the true value, causing an error in the carbon-to-hydrogen ratio. You can review this concept in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

steps.title: Physical collection difficulties
steps.content:
Another procedural limitation involves the **collection of gaseous products**. In a laboratory setup, gases like $\text{CO}_2$ and water vapor must be passed through absorption tubes. 

Common physical issues include:
*   **Gas leaks**: Small gaps in the apparatus allow the produced gases to escape before they reach the weighing scale.
*   **Incomplete absorption**: The chemicals used to trap the gases (like anhydrous $\text{CaCl}_2$ or $\text{NaOH}$) might not absorb 100% of the products passing through.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you only need to state one of these problems. 

One major problem is the **formation of soot or carbon monoxide due to incomplete combustion**.

topic.title: R2.1 How much? The amount of chemical change
